description: Warm up your fingers with basic hand stretches and the 1-2-3-4 chromatic exercise on the first 4 frets.
action: Do hand stretches (30 sec), then play the 1-2-3-4 chromatic pattern on each string, frets 1-4, starting from the low E string.
context: Warming up prevents injury and builds finger independence. The 1-2-3-4 exercise is the foundation of all left-hand technique — one finger per fret.
constraint_note: Play slowly. Each note should ring clearly before moving to the next. If a note buzzes, press harder and closer to the fret wire.
example: Low E string: index on fret 1, middle on fret 2, ring on fret 3, pinky on fret 4. Then move to A string. Repeat all 6 strings.

description: Practice the 4-3-2-1 reverse chromatic exercise descending on each string.
action: Play 4-3-2-1 on each string from high E down to low E. Start with pinky on fret 4, lift one finger at a time.
context: The reverse pattern trains your pinky to lead — most beginners avoid their pinky. This builds balanced finger strength.
constraint_note: Keep all 4 fingers hovering close to the fretboard. Don't let your pinky fly away after lifting it.
example: High E string: pinky fret 4, ring fret 3, middle fret 2, index fret 1. Move down to B string. Repeat.

description: Finger stretching exercises away from the guitar — build flexibility for chord shapes.
action: Do these 4 hand exercises: (1) Spread fingers wide, hold 5 sec. (2) Make fists, hold 5 sec. (3) Rotate wrists — 5 circles each direction. (4) Gently pull each finger back toward the back of your hand, hold 3 sec each.
context: Finger flexibility directly affects how easily you can form chords and reach stretches on the fretboard. These exercises can be done anywhere.
constraint_note: Be gentle — never force a stretch to the point of pain. Do 3 rounds of each exercise.
example: Round 1: spread (5s) → fists (5s) → wrist circles (10 each) → finger pulls (3s each × 4 fingers × 2 hands). Repeat 2 more times.

steps: Spread all fingers wide, hold 5 seconds, release
Make tight fists, hold 5 seconds, release
Rotate wrists in circles — 5 clockwise, 5 counterclockwise
Gently pull each finger back toward the back of your hand, hold 3 seconds each
Repeat all 4 exercises 3 times

description: Practice alternate picking on a single open string to build right-hand consistency.
action: Pick the open low E string with strict down-up-down-up alternate picking. Stay perfectly in time with the metronome — one pick stroke per beat.
context: Alternate picking is the most fundamental right-hand technique. Mastering it on one string first builds the muscle memory before adding left-hand complexity.
constraint_note: Every downstroke lands on the beat. Every upstroke lands on the 'and'. Keep your pick angle consistent. Don't tense your wrist.
example: At 60 BPM: DOWN (beat 1) UP (and) DOWN (beat 2) UP (and) DOWN (beat 3) UP (and) DOWN (beat 4) UP (and). Repeat for 2 minutes, then try on the A string.

description: Practice fretting hand pressure — find the minimum pressure needed for a clean note.
action: On any string, fret 5: start with zero pressure and gradually increase until the note rings clean. Find the exact minimum pressure. Repeat on all 6 strings.
context: Most beginners press way too hard, causing hand fatigue and slowing chord changes. Learning minimum pressure transforms your endurance and speed.
constraint_note: Press just behind the fret wire (toward the headstock), not on top of it. The sweet spot requires surprisingly little pressure.
example: Start with your finger barely touching the string — you'll hear a muted thud. Slowly add pressure. The moment it rings clean, stop. That's your target pressure.

description: Practice the 1-2-3-4 chromatic exercise across strings — build coordination between both hands.
action: Play 1-2-3-4 on the low E string, then without pausing play 1-2-3-4 on the A string, continuing up to the high E. Then reverse back down.
context: String crossing — moving cleanly between strings — is where most beginners stumble. This exercise trains smooth transitions.
constraint_note: When crossing to a new string, the pick should already be in position. No pauses between strings. Keep the tempo steady.

description: Thumb position check — practice proper thumb placement behind the neck.
action: Place your thumb on the back of the neck, roughly behind fret 2. It should point upward, not wrap over the top. Play the 1-2-3-4 exercise while maintaining this thumb position.
context: Thumb position is the invisible foundation of good technique. A thumb that wraps over the top limits your finger reach and causes wrist strain.
constraint_note: Your thumb should be roughly opposite your middle finger. Check it every 30 seconds — it will drift. The wrist should be slightly forward, not collapsed.
example: Good: thumb pointing up, pad of thumb touching the neck center. Bad: thumb hooked over the top edge, or thumb flat against the neck.

description: Practice pick grip and angle — find a comfortable, consistent picking position.
action: Hold the pick between your thumb and the side of your index finger. Only 2-3mm of the pick tip should be visible. Strum all 6 strings slowly, then pick individual strings. Adjust until it feels natural.
context: Pick grip affects tone, speed, and comfort. Too much pick exposed = sloppy sound. Too little = scratchy. Finding your sweet spot early saves months of frustration.
constraint_note: The pick should be firm but not death-gripped. Your hand should be relaxed. Try picking near the sound hole, then near the bridge — hear the tone difference.
example: Grip test: strum a chord. If the pick flies out, grip tighter. If your hand cramps after 30 seconds, loosen up. The pick should feel like an extension of your finger.

description: Spider walk exercise — build finger independence with a diagonal pattern across strings.
action: Play the spider walk: frets 1-2-3-4 on the low E, then shift one fret up and play 2-3-4-5 on the A string, then 3-4-5-6 on D, continuing diagonally up the neck and across strings.
context: The spider walk forces each finger to operate independently while your hand shifts position. It's the single best exercise for finger independence.
constraint_note: Keep fingers you're not using hovering close to the fretboard. Each note must ring clearly. If you hear buzzing, slow down.
example: E: 1-2-3-4 | A: 2-3-4-5 | D: 3-4-5-6 | G: 4-5-6-7 | B: 5-6-7-8 | e: 6-7-8-9. Then reverse the pattern coming back down.

description: String skipping exercise — pick non-adjacent strings to improve right-hand accuracy.
action: Play fret 5 on the low E string, then skip to fret 5 on the D string, then back to A string fret 5, then skip to G string fret 5. Continue the skip pattern across all strings.
context: String skipping trains your picking hand to jump accurately. It's essential for arpeggios, lead playing, and any non-linear melody.
constraint_note: Use alternate picking throughout. The skip should be clean — don't accidentally hit the string in between. Start very slowly.
example: Pattern: E(5) → D(5) → A(5) → G(5) → D(5) → B(5) → G(5) → e(5). All on fret 5 to isolate the right hand.

description: Practice the 1-3-2-4 permutation exercise for advanced finger independence.
action: Instead of 1-2-3-4, play 1-3-2-4 on each string. Index, ring, middle, pinky. This breaks the natural sequential pattern and forces true independence.
context: Your fingers naturally want to move in order. Permutation exercises break that habit and build the independence needed for complex chord shapes and lead lines.
constraint_note: This will feel awkward — that's the point. Go extremely slowly. Each note must be clean. Don't let fingers move together.
example: Low E: fret 1 (index) → fret 3 (ring) → fret 2 (middle) → fret 4 (pinky). Move to A string. Repeat all 6 strings.

description: Alternate picking speed builder — gradually increase tempo on a single-string exercise.
action: Play the 1-2-3-4 pattern on the A string with alternate picking. Start at 60 BPM, play for 1 minute. Increase by 10 BPM. Repeat until you can't play cleanly, then drop back 10 BPM and stay there.
context: Speed comes from accuracy at slow tempos, gradually pushed faster. Jumping to fast tempos builds sloppy habits that are hard to fix later.
constraint_note: The moment notes start buzzing or your timing slips, you've gone too fast. Drop back. Clean at 80 BPM beats sloppy at 120.
example: 60 BPM (1 min) → 70 BPM (1 min) → 80 BPM (1 min) → 90 BPM (1 min) → if sloppy, back to 80 BPM (2 min).

description: Practice hammer-ons on each string — build left-hand striking power.
action: Pick fret 5 on the low E string, then hammer-on to fret 7 without picking again. The hammered note should ring as loud as the picked note. Repeat on all 6 strings.
context: Hammer-ons are the foundation of legato playing. The key is striking the fret with the fingertip, not just placing it down. It's a snap, not a press.
constraint_note: The hammer-on note must be as loud as the picked note. If it's quiet, you're placing your finger instead of striking. Use your wrist, not just the finger.
example: E: pick 5, hammer 7 | A: pick 5, hammer 7 | D: pick 5, hammer 7 | Continue on all strings. Then try 5-to-8 (wider stretch).

description: Practice pull-offs on each string — the reverse of hammer-ons.
action: Fret both fret 5 and fret 7 on the low E string. Pick once, then pull off from fret 7 to fret 5 — your finger should pluck the string slightly as it lifts. Repeat on all strings.
context: Pull-offs complete the legato toolkit. The trick is that your finger doesn't just lift — it pulls slightly downward (toward the floor) as it releases, plucking the string.
constraint_note: The pull-off note must ring clearly. If it's dead, you're lifting straight up instead of pulling. Think of it as a tiny pluck with your fretting finger.
example: E: fret 5+7, pick, pull off 7→5 | A: same | D: same | Continue all strings. Then try hammer-on + pull-off combo: pick 5, hammer 7, pull off 5.

description: Hammer-on / pull-off trill exercise — build legato endurance.
action: On the B string, fret 5: pick once, then continuously hammer-on to fret 7 and pull-off back to fret 5 as fast as you can while staying even. Sustain for 15 seconds. Rest. Repeat on each string.
context: Trills build the endurance and speed needed for fluid legato playing. They're also a great workout for finger strength.
constraint_note: Keep the volume even between hammer-ons and pull-offs. If the pull-offs are quieter, focus on the pulling motion. Time each trill — aim for 15 seconds sustained.
example: B string: pick fret 5 → hammer 7 → pull 5 → hammer 7 → pull 5... (15 sec). Rest 10 sec. Move to G string. Repeat.

description: Cross-string legato — hammer-ons and pull-offs moving across strings.
action: Play this legato pattern: pick fret 5 on E, hammer 7, hammer 8. Move to A string: pick 5, hammer 7, hammer 8. Continue across all 6 strings without stopping.
context: Cross-string legato combines left-hand technique with smooth string transitions. It's the basis of fluid lead guitar playing.
constraint_note: Only pick the first note on each string — the other two are hammered. The string change should be seamless. No gaps, no extra picks.
example: E: pick 5, hammer 7, hammer 8 → A: pick 5, hammer 7, hammer 8 → D: pick 5, hammer 7, hammer 8 → continue up, then reverse coming back down.

description: Economy of motion exercise — minimize finger movement distance.
action: Play the 1-2-3-4 exercise at a slow tempo. Focus entirely on how far your fingers lift off the fretboard after each note. They should hover no more than 5mm above the strings.
context: Excess finger movement is the #1 speed killer. Reducing lift distance by even a few millimeters dramatically increases your maximum speed.
constraint_note: This is a mindfulness exercise. Watch your fingers. After lifting, they should barely clear the string. If your pinky flies up to the ceiling, consciously pull it back down.
example: Play at 40 BPM. After each note, check: is the finger that just played hovering close? Is the next finger already in position? Think 'lazy fingers' — minimum effort, minimum distance.

description: Slide exercise — practice sliding between frets smoothly.
action: On the G string: pick fret 5, slide up to fret 7 (maintain pressure during the slide). Then pick fret 7, slide up to fret 9. Practice sliding up and down on each string.
context: Slides add expressiveness to your playing and are essential for blues, rock, and lead guitar. The key is maintaining consistent pressure throughout the slide.
constraint_note: The note should ring continuously during the slide — no dead spots. Don't lift pressure. Don't re-pick at the destination fret. One pick, one slide.
example: G string: pick 5 → slide to 7 | pick 7 → slide to 9 | pick 9 → slide to 12 | Then reverse: pick 12 → slide to 9 → slide to 7 → slide to 5.

description: Bending exercise — practice half-step and whole-step bends on the B and G strings.
action: On the B string fret 8: pick the note, then bend up a whole step (should match the sound of fret 10). Use your ring finger backed by middle and index. Repeat 10 times. Then try on the G string fret 7.
context: Bending is what makes guitar expressive — it's how you 'sing' with the instrument. Accurate bending (hitting the target pitch) separates good players from great ones.
constraint_note: Push the string toward the ceiling (on B and high E strings). Use 3 fingers together for strength. Check your bend: play fret 10 first, memorize the pitch, then bend fret 8 to match it.
example: B string: play fret 10 (target pitch) → now play fret 8 and bend up until it matches → hold the bend for 2 seconds → release slowly. Repeat 10 times.

description: Vibrato exercise — add controlled vibrato to sustained notes.
action: Fret the B string at fret 8. Pick the note and add vibrato — a rhythmic, slight bend-and-release motion. Aim for even, controlled oscillation. Practice on different strings and frets.
context: Vibrato is your 'signature sound' — every great guitarist has a distinctive vibrato. It adds life to sustained notes and is the difference between a note that sings and one that just sits there.
constraint_note: Vibrato should be even and rhythmic, not random wobbling. Start slow — bend slightly up, release, bend up, release — in time with a slow pulse. Width and speed come later.
example: B string fret 8: pick → bend up slightly → release → bend → release → bend → release. Each cycle should take about 1 second. Do 10 cycles, rest, repeat.

description: 3-note-per-string chromatic exercise for building speed across the full neck.
action: Play 3 notes per string chromatically: frets 5-6-7 on E, 5-6-7 on A, continue across all strings. Then shift up one fret and repeat at 6-7-8. Cover frets 1-12.
context: Three-note-per-string patterns are the basis of fast scale runs. This chromatic version isolates the picking pattern without worrying about scale shapes.
constraint_note: Use strict alternate picking. The picking pattern reverses on each string (down-up-down on E, up-down-up on A). This is the key to speed.

description: Left-hand muting exercise — practice muting strings you're not playing.
action: Play a note on the D string. While it rings, check: are the other 5 strings silent? Lightly touch adjacent strings with unused fingers to mute them. Practice on each string.
context: Clean playing means only the intended strings ring. Muting unwanted strings is what separates a clean player from a noisy one, especially with distortion.
constraint_note: After fretting a note, strum all 6 strings. Only the fretted note should sound. Use your fretting fingers and the underside of your index finger to mute neighbors.

description: Pinky strengthening drill — exercises specifically targeting the weakest finger.
action: Play this pattern on each string: index fret 5, pinky fret 8 (skip frets 6-7). Pick each note twice. The stretch between index and pinky builds strength and reach.
context: The pinky is most guitarists' weakest finger. Targeted exercises close the strength gap and unlock chord shapes and scale positions that require pinky reach.
constraint_note: Keep your index finger planted on fret 5 while your pinky reaches for fret 8. Don't shift your whole hand. The stretch should feel challenging but not painful.

description: Mental fretboard visualization — practice seeing the fretboard in your mind without a guitar.
action: Close your eyes. Visualize the guitar fretboard. Picture playing a G chord — see each finger pressing down on the correct fret and string. Now visualize switching to C chord. See the finger movement in slow motion.
context: Mental practice activates the same neural pathways as physical practice. Studies show musicians who combine mental and physical practice improve faster than those who only practice physically.
constraint_note: Be specific in your visualization — see the exact fret numbers, feel the string tension, hear the chord ring out. Vague visualization doesn't help. Detailed visualization does.
example: G chord: ring finger on low E fret 3, middle finger on A fret 2, pinky on high E fret 3. See it. Feel it. Now switch to C: ring on A fret 3, middle on D fret 2, index on B fret 1.

description: Coordination challenge — play different rhythms with each hand simultaneously.
action: Fret the 1-2-3-4 pattern on the low E string. Pick each note once (quarter notes). Now try: pick each note twice (eighth notes) while your left hand still moves at quarter-note speed. Then try 3 picks per fret (triplets).
context: Hand independence — each hand doing something different — is what makes complex guitar playing possible. This exercise directly trains that separation.
constraint_note: Start with quarter notes until solid. Then double the picking speed while keeping the fretting hand at the same pace. The hands must be independent.

description: Learn the Em chord — the easiest guitar chord.
action: Place your middle finger on the A string fret 2 and ring finger on the D string fret 2. Strum all 6 strings. This is Em. Strum it 20 times, lifting your fingers and replacing them each time.
context: Em is the first chord most guitarists learn because it only uses 2 fingers and all 6 strings ring. It appears in thousands of songs across every genre.
constraint_note: Every string should ring clearly. After strumming, pick each string individually to check — if any buzz, adjust your finger position closer to the fret wire.
example: Finger placement: middle finger on A string fret 2, ring finger on D string fret 2. All other strings are open (unfretted). Strum from low E to high E.

description: Learn the Am chord and practice switching between Em and Am.
action: Learn Am: index on B fret 1, middle on D fret 2, ring on G fret 2. Strum strings 5-1 (skip low E). Then practice switching between Em and Am — 4 strums each, back and forth.
context: Am is the second essential chord. The Em→Am switch is one of the easiest chord changes because your middle finger stays on the same fret.
constraint_note: When switching from Em to Am, notice that your middle finger barely moves. Use it as an anchor. The low E string should NOT be strummed on Am.

description: Learn the C major chord.
action: Learn C: ring finger on A fret 3, middle on D fret 2, index on B fret 1. Strum strings 5-1. Practice placing and lifting the chord shape 15 times.
context: C major is one of the most common chords in popular music. It's also your first 3-finger chord, which builds the foundation for more complex shapes.
constraint_note: The low E string should not ring. Your ring finger on A fret 3 can lightly touch the low E to mute it. Check each string individually for clean sound.

description: Learn the G major chord — your first chord using all 6 strings with 3 fingers.
action: Learn G: ring finger on low E fret 3, middle finger on A fret 2, pinky on high E fret 3. Strum all 6 strings. Practice forming the shape 15 times.
context: G major uses all 6 strings and requires your pinky — a big step up. It's in countless songs and pairs naturally with C, D, and Em.
constraint_note: The pinky on high E fret 3 is the hard part. If it mutes the B string, arch your finger more. All 6 strings should ring clearly.

description: Learn the D major chord.
action: Learn D: index on G fret 2, ring on B fret 3, middle on high E fret 2. Strum strings 4-1 only (skip E and A). Practice 15 times.
context: D major completes your first set of essential open chords. With Em, Am, C, G, and D you can play hundreds of songs.
constraint_note: Only strum the top 4 strings (D, G, B, high E). The low E and A strings should not ring. Your thumb can mute the low E by lightly touching it.

description: Learn the E major chord.
action: Learn E: index on G fret 1, middle on A fret 2, ring on D fret 2. Strum all 6 strings. Notice it's the same shape as Am but shifted one string toward the bass.
context: E major is a powerful, full-sounding chord that uses all 6 strings. It's the foundation of rock, blues, and country music.
constraint_note: All 6 strings should ring. This chord is similar to Am — if you know Am, just shift the whole shape one string toward the bass strings.

description: Learn the A major chord.
action: Learn A: index on D fret 2, middle on G fret 2, ring on B fret 2. All three fingers on fret 2, strings D-G-B. Strum strings 5-1.
context: A major is compact — three fingers all on the same fret. It pairs with E and D for classic rock and country progressions.
constraint_note: Don't strum the low E string. Your fingers are crowded on fret 2 — make sure each finger is arched enough not to mute its neighbor strings.

description: One-minute chord changes: Em ↔ Am. Build speed on the easiest chord switch.
action: Set a timer for 1 minute. Switch between Em and Am as many times as you can, strumming each chord once. Count your changes. Rest 30 seconds. Repeat 3 times. Try to beat your count each round.
context: Chord change speed is the #1 skill that separates beginners from players who can play songs. One-minute change drills are the fastest way to build this skill.
constraint_note: Every chord must sound clean — don't sacrifice quality for speed. If a chord buzzes, it doesn't count. Aim for 30+ clean changes per minute.

description: One-minute chord changes: G ↔ C. Practice the most common chord switch in pop music.
action: Set a timer for 1 minute. Switch between G and C, strumming once per chord. Count clean changes. Rest. Repeat 3 times.
context: G to C is everywhere — from Beatles to Taylor Swift. The switch is tricky because all three fingers move. Finding anchor fingers (middle finger stays near fret 2) helps.
constraint_note: Look for the pivot: your ring finger moves from E fret 3 to A fret 3 — same fret, adjacent string. Use that as your guide finger.

description: One-minute chord changes: C ↔ D. Practice a common but tricky switch.
action: One-minute drill: switch between C and D, strumming once per chord. Count clean changes. The challenge: these chords share no common finger positions.
context: C to D requires all fingers to move to completely different positions. This is where most beginners slow down. Drilling it builds the muscle memory.
constraint_note: Visualize the target chord shape before you move. Move all fingers simultaneously, not one at a time. Aim for 20+ clean changes per minute.

description: Play the G-C-D chord progression — the backbone of thousands of songs.
action: Play G (4 strums) → C (4 strums) → D (4 strums) → C (4 strums). Repeat this loop for 5 minutes with the metronome. Each strum on the beat.
context: G-C-D is arguably the most common chord progression in Western music. Songs like Sweet Home Alabama, Twist and Shout, and countless others use it.
constraint_note: Keep the rhythm steady — don't slow down during chord changes. If you need to, slow the metronome until changes are seamless, then speed up.

description: Play the Am-G-F-E progression — a classic minor key progression.
action: Play Am (4 strums) → G (4 strums) → F (4 strums) → E (4 strums). The F chord is a mini-barre: index barres strings 1-2 at fret 1, middle on G fret 2, ring on D fret 3.
context: This descending minor progression appears in Stairway to Heaven, Hit the Road Jack, and many Spanish/flamenco pieces. The F chord is your first barre challenge.
constraint_note: The F chord is hard. Use the 'easy F' first: just fret strings 1-2 with your index at fret 1, middle on G2, ring on D3. Skip the low E and A strings.

description: Learn the F major barre chord — the gateway to playing in any key.
action: Learn F barre: index finger barres ALL 6 strings at fret 1. Middle on G fret 2, ring on A fret 3, pinky on D fret 3. Strum all 6 strings. Practice forming and releasing 10 times.
context: The F barre chord is the biggest hurdle for beginners. Once you master it, you can play any major chord by moving this shape up the neck. It's the key that unlocks the entire fretboard.
constraint_note: The barre is the hard part. Roll your index finger slightly to use the bony edge, not the flat pad. Press close to the fret wire. It takes weeks to get clean — be patient.

description: Practice the E-shape barre chord at different fret positions.
action: The F barre shape is an 'E shape' moved up. Play it at fret 1 (F), fret 3 (G), fret 5 (A), fret 7 (B), fret 8 (C). Hold each for 4 strums, then slide to the next.
context: One barre chord shape gives you 12 different chords just by moving it up the neck. This is why barre chords are so powerful — one shape, every key.
constraint_note: All 6 strings must ring at each position. The higher up the neck you go, the easier the barre becomes (frets are closer together). If fret 1 is too hard, start at fret 5.

description: Learn the Bm barre chord — the A-shape barre.
action: Learn Bm: index barres all strings at fret 2. Ring finger barres strings D-G-B at fret 4. Pinky on B fret 4 (or ring finger covers it). Strum strings 5-1.
context: Bm is the second essential barre chord shape (A-shape). Combined with the E-shape barre, you can now play any major or minor chord anywhere on the neck.
constraint_note: Don't strum the low E string. The A-shape barre is different from the E-shape — your ring finger does a mini-barre across 3 strings. This takes practice.

description: One-minute chord changes with barre chords: F ↔ C.
action: One-minute drill: switch between F barre and open C. Count clean changes. This trains the transition between barre and open chord shapes.
context: Real songs mix barre and open chords constantly. The F↔C switch is one of the most common and trains you to release and reform the barre quickly.
constraint_note: When releasing the barre, don't let your hand fly away from the neck. Keep it close. The faster you can reform the barre, the smoother your playing.

description: Play the I-V-vi-IV progression in G: G-D-Em-C — the most popular progression in modern music.
action: Play G (4 strums) → D (4 strums) → Em (4 strums) → C (4 strums). Loop for 5 minutes. This is the progression behind Let It Be, No Woman No Cry, With or Without You, and hundreds more.
context: The I-V-vi-IV progression is statistically the most common in pop music. Learning to play it smoothly in G means you can already accompany dozens of songs.
constraint_note: Focus on making the chord changes invisible — the rhythm should never stop or slow down for a chord change. If it does, slow the metronome.

description: Learn 7th chords: Em7, Am7, Cmaj7, G7, D7.
action: Learn these 7th chords: Em7 (Em but lift ring finger), Am7 (Am but lift ring finger off G string), Cmaj7 (C but lift index off B1), G7 (G shape + index on high E fret 1), D7 (D but index on B fret 1). Practice each 5 times.
context: 7th chords add color and sophistication. They're essential for blues, jazz, and soul. Most are just one finger different from chords you already know.
constraint_note: Compare each 7th chord to its basic version. Play Em, then Em7 — hear the difference. The 7th adds a slightly jazzy, unresolved quality.

description: Play a 12-bar blues in E: E-A-B7 — the foundation of blues guitar.
action: Play the 12-bar blues: E(4 bars) → A(2 bars) → E(2 bars) → B7(1 bar) → A(1 bar) → E(1 bar) → B7(1 bar). Each bar = 4 strums. Loop it.
context: The 12-bar blues is the most important chord progression in blues, rock, and jazz. Every guitarist should be able to play it in their sleep.
constraint_note: B7: index on D fret 1, middle on A fret 2, ring on G fret 2, pinky on high E fret 2. Keep the rhythm steady — blues lives and dies by its groove.

description: Practice power chords — the foundation of rock guitar.
action: Learn the power chord shape: index on any root note, ring finger 2 frets up on the next string. Play E5 (E fret 0 + A fret 2), A5 (A fret 0 + D fret 2), then moveable: index on E fret 3 + ring on A fret 5 = G5.
context: Power chords are the backbone of rock, punk, and metal. They're neither major nor minor — just root and fifth — which gives them a raw, powerful sound.
constraint_note: Only strum the 2 strings you're fretting (plus optionally the octave on the next string). Mute everything else. Power chords should sound tight and punchy.

description: Learn sus2 and sus4 chords: Asus2, Asus4, Dsus2, Dsus4.
action: Learn: Asus2 (A but lift middle finger), Asus4 (A + pinky on G fret 3), Dsus2 (D but lift index), Dsus4 (D + pinky on high E fret 3). Practice the 'sus resolve': Asus4 → A → Asus2 → A.
context: Sus chords create tension that resolves beautifully back to the major chord. The sus4→major→sus2→major pattern is used in countless songs for emotional movement.
constraint_note: Listen to the tension in the sus chord and the resolution when you return to the major. This push-and-pull is what makes sus chords musical, not just theoretical.

description: Practice chord transitions with a metronome — change on beat 1 of every bar.
action: Pick any 4 chords you know. Play each for one bar (4 beats). The chord change MUST happen exactly on beat 1 — not before, not after. Use the metronome to enforce this.
context: In real music, chord changes happen on specific beats. Training yourself to change exactly on the beat — not rushing or dragging — is what makes you sound like a musician, not a student.
constraint_note: If you can't change in time, the tempo is too fast. Slow down until the change lands perfectly on beat 1. Then gradually increase.

description: Learn add9 chords: Cadd9, Gadd9 — the 'pretty' open chords.
action: Learn Cadd9: C chord + pinky on high E fret 3. Learn Gadd9: G chord but middle finger on B fret 3 instead of A fret 2 (keep ring on E3, pinky on e3). Practice switching between them.
context: Add9 chords are the 'upgraded' versions of C and G. They sound fuller and more modern. Many singer-songwriters use Cadd9 and G interchangeably with C and G.
constraint_note: Cadd9 is just C with your pinky added. Gadd9 requires a different fingering than standard G — practice the new finger positions.

description: Play the I-V-vi-IV in different keys using barre chords.
action: Play I-V-vi-IV in 3 keys: Key of G (G-D-Em-C, open), Key of A (A-E-F#m-D, mix of open and barre), Key of C (C-G-Am-F, with F barre). 2 minutes per key.
context: Being able to play the same progression in multiple keys is essential for playing with other musicians and singing in your comfortable range.
constraint_note: The barre chords should sound as clean as the open chords. If they don't, spend extra time on the keys that use barres.

description: Learn diminished and augmented triads — the 'spicy' chords.
action: Learn Bdim: index B fret 1, ring D fret 3, middle G fret 2, pinky high E fret 1. Learn Caug: C shape but move ring finger to A fret 3 and add pinky to G fret 1 (or use the shape: x-3-2-1-1-0). Play each 5 times and listen to their unique tension.
context: Diminished chords sound tense and unstable. Augmented chords sound dreamy and unresolved. Both are used as passing chords to create movement between stable chords.
constraint_note: These chords sound 'wrong' on their own — that's intentional. Play Bdim then resolve to C. Play Caug then resolve to F. Hear how the tension releases.

description: Chord melody exercise — play a melody on the high strings while holding a chord shape.
action: Hold a C chord. While keeping the shape, add your pinky to high E fret 3, then lift it. Then add it to B fret 3, then lift. You're playing a melody over a held chord. Try with G and Am too.
context: Chord melody is the foundation of solo guitar playing — bass notes and melody happening simultaneously. It starts with adding single notes to chord shapes you already know.
constraint_note: The base chord must keep ringing while you add and remove the melody note. Don't lift your other fingers. This requires finger independence.

description: Practice chord voicings up the neck — same chord, different positions.
action: Play C major in 3 positions: open C (fret 0-3), C barre at fret 3 (A-shape), C barre at fret 8 (E-shape). Listen to how the same chord sounds different in each position.
context: The fretboard has multiple voicings for every chord. Knowing 2-3 positions for common chords lets you choose the voicing that sounds best for each song and minimizes hand movement.
constraint_note: Each voicing should sound clean. Compare the brightness of open C vs the fullness of barre C at fret 8. Different voicings suit different musical contexts.

description: Chord quiz — name the chord from its fingering description.
action: Test your chord knowledge: for each fingering, name the chord without looking it up. Then verify by playing it.
context: Knowing chord shapes by name — not just by muscle memory — helps you communicate with other musicians and understand song charts.

items.prompt: Fingers on A2, D2, all strings open: ___
items.hint: A minor-sounding 2-finger chord

items.prompt: Index B1, middle D2, ring G2, strum 5 strings: ___
items.hint: The minor version of A

items.prompt: Ring A3, middle D2, index B1, strum 5 strings: ___
items.hint: One of the most common major chords

items.prompt: Ring E3, middle A2, pinky e3, all 6 strings: ___
items.hint: Uses all 6 strings with the pinky

items.prompt: Index G2, ring B3, middle e2, strum 4 strings: ___
items.hint: Only uses the top 4 strings

items.prompt: Index G1, middle A2, ring D2, all 6 strings: ___
items.hint: Full 6-string major chord, similar shape to Am

description: Chord progression ear training — identify whether a progression sounds major or minor.
action: Play these progressions and label each as 'happy/major' or 'sad/minor': (1) G-C-D-C, (2) Am-F-C-G, (3) Em-C-G-D, (4) D-A-Bm-G. Play each twice and decide.
context: Recognizing the emotional quality of chord progressions is the first step toward playing by ear and writing your own music.
constraint_note: Don't overthink it — just listen to the overall feeling. Major progressions feel bright and resolved. Minor progressions feel darker or more emotional.

description: Staccato chord practice — play short, punchy chords with precise muting.
action: Play any chord with a sharp downstroke, then immediately mute all strings by releasing fretting pressure (don't lift fingers off strings). The chord should ring for less than half a second. Practice on G, C, D, Am.
context: Staccato chords are essential for funk, reggae, and rhythmic playing. The muting technique — releasing pressure without lifting fingers — is a skill in itself.
constraint_note: The mute must be instant and complete. No strings should ring after you release pressure. Your fretting hand does the muting, not your strumming hand.

description: Learn the open string names: E-A-D-G-B-E.
action: Memorize the open string names from thickest to thinnest: E-A-D-G-B-E. Use the mnemonic 'Eddie Ate Dynamite Good Bye Eddie'. Play each open string and say its name aloud 5 times.
context: Knowing string names is the absolute foundation. Every chord diagram, tab, and music conversation assumes you know which string is which.
constraint_note: Say the name OUT LOUD as you play each string. Hearing + saying + playing creates stronger memory than just reading.

items.body: The low E string is your anchor — it's the deepest note on the guitar and the root of countless riffs and bass lines. When tuning, always start here. The string should vibrate slowly enough that you can almost see it move. Mnemonic for all strings: 'Eddie Ate Dynamite Good Bye Eddie' (E-A-D-G-B-E). The low E and high E are the same note two octaves apart.

The A string is where most barre chords and power chords are rooted. When you play an A-shaped barre chord, this string carries the root note. It's also the reference string for tuning: the 5th fret of the low E should match the open A string. If you're learning bass lines, the A string is where most of the action happens in rock and pop music.

The D string sits right in the middle of the guitar's range — it's the workhorse string for rhythm playing. Open D is the root of the D major chord, one of the first chords beginners learn. Many folk and country songs use open D tuning (DADF#AD) for a rich, resonant sound. When fingerpicking, your middle finger typically handles this string.

The G string is the most problematic string for tuning — it's where the guitar's intonation issues are most noticeable. It's also the thinnest wound string (on acoustic) or the thickest plain string (on electric), which makes it feel different from its neighbors. Many guitarists find the G string goes out of tune fastest. Tip: when tuning, always tune UP to the note, never down — this keeps tension consistent.

The B string is crucial for chord voicings — it often carries the 'color' notes that make chords sound major, minor, or jazzy. It's a plain (unwound) steel string, so it has a brighter, thinner tone than the wound strings below it. The B string is offset from the pattern of the other strings: while most adjacent strings are tuned in 4ths (5 frets apart), G to B is a major 3rd (4 frets). This irregularity is why chord shapes shift when they cross the G-B boundary.

The high E string is where melodies live. It's the same note as the 6th string but two octaves higher. Because it's the thinnest string, it's the easiest to bend (push sideways to raise the pitch) — bending on the high E is fundamental to blues and rock soloing. It's also the most fragile string and breaks most often. Keep spares. When strumming, the high E adds sparkle and brightness to chords.

description: Learn the natural notes on the low E string.
action: Learn these notes on the low E string: open=E, fret 1=F, fret 3=G, fret 5=A, fret 7=B, fret 8=C, fret 10=D, fret 12=E (octave). Play each and say the note name.
context: The low E string is your roadmap. Once you know it, you know where every barre chord root lives. Fret 3=G chord, fret 5=A chord, fret 7=B chord, etc.
constraint_note: Notice the pattern: most notes are 2 frets apart, except E-F and B-C which are only 1 fret apart. This is the most important pattern in music.

items.body: Open E is the lowest standard note on the guitar. It's the root of E major and E minor — two of the most common guitar chords. Many iconic riffs start on open E: the opening of 'Smoke on the Water' (sort of), 'Iron Man,' and countless metal songs. When you're lost on the fretboard, the open E is home base. Learn to hear this pitch and you'll always be able to orient yourself.

F is just one fret up from open E — a half step. The F note on the first fret is important because F major is one of the hardest beginner chords (it requires a barre). Knowing that F lives here helps you understand WHY the barre chord shape works. This is also where the famous 'Jaws' theme starts — that menacing half-step between E and F.

G on the third fret is a power note — it's the root of G major, one of the most common keys in popular music. The distance from E to G (3 frets) is a minor third interval, which has a dark, moody sound. Many blues riffs use the E-G movement. Tip: the 3rd fret on the low E string is also where you'd put your finger for a G power chord (G5).

A on the fifth fret is a crucial reference point: it should sound exactly like the open A string (5th string). This is the most basic tuning check. The 5th fret = next string relationship works for all strings except G to B (which is 4th fret). Memorize this: 5th fret of any string = the open string below it (except G→B which is 4th fret).

B on the seventh fret. The distance from A (fret 5) to B (fret 7) is a whole step — two frets. B is the fifth of E, which means playing E and B together sounds powerful and stable. This is the basis of power chords: root + fifth. Every time you play a power chord, you're using this root-fifth relationship.

C on the eighth fret. C is one of the most important notes in music — it's the 'home base' of music theory because the C major scale has no sharps or flats. On the low E string, C at fret 8 is useful for walking bass lines and for understanding how the fretboard connects to music theory. The distance from B (fret 7) to C (fret 8) is a half step — B and C are natural half steps, just like E and F.

D on the tenth fret. You're getting high up the neck now. D is the root of D major — but up here, it's an octave higher than the open D string (4th string). This equivalence is useful: any note you know on one string exists on other strings at different frets. Learning the fretboard means building a mental map of where the same note appears in different positions.

E on the twelfth fret — you've come full circle. The 12th fret is ALWAYS the same note as the open string, one octave higher. This is true for every string. The 12th fret is marked with double dots on most guitars for exactly this reason. Everything above fret 12 repeats the pattern from frets 0-11. If you know the first 12 frets, you know the entire fretboard.

description: Learn the natural notes on the A string.
action: Learn notes on the A string: open=A, fret 2=B, fret 3=C, fret 5=D, fret 7=E, fret 8=F, fret 10=G, fret 12=A. Play each and say the name.
context: The A string is where A-shape barre chord roots live. Knowing A string notes means you can instantly find any minor barre chord position.
constraint_note: Same pattern as the E string but starting on A. B-C (frets 2-3) and E-F (frets 7-8) are 1 fret apart. Everything else is 2 frets apart.

items.body: Open A is the root of A major and A minor — two essential chords. The A note is the universal tuning reference: orchestras tune to A440 (A at 440 Hz). Your open A string should be 110 Hz (two octaves below concert A). Many tuner apps show the frequency, which helps you understand the physics of pitch. A is also the starting point for the A minor pentatonic scale — the first scale most guitarists learn for soloing.

B on the second fret of the A string. This is where B-rooted barre chords live — B major and B minor barre chords start here. B is a common key in pop music (especially with a capo). The distance from A to B (2 frets = whole step) is the same as E to F# or D to E. Recognizing these whole-step intervals helps you navigate the fretboard by sound, not just by memorized positions.

C on the third fret of the A string is one of the most important notes to memorize. It's the root position for C-shaped barre chords and the starting point for the C major scale in this position. Since C major has no sharps or flats, it's the 'default' key for understanding music theory. Many guitarists learn the fretboard by first memorizing all the C positions across all strings.

D on the fifth fret — same tuning trick as before: 5th fret of A should match the open D string. D is the root of D major, a bright and popular key for acoustic guitar. This position is also where D-rooted barre chords begin. Notice the pattern: 5th fret always gives you the next string's open note (except G→B). This single rule lets you find notes quickly across the fretboard.

E on the seventh fret of the A string. This is the same note as the open high E string (two octaves lower) and the open low E string (one octave higher). The 7th fret is a perfect fifth above the open string — this relationship is the basis of power chords. Knowing E lives here helps you build E-rooted chords and riffs in higher positions.

F on the eighth fret. F is a tricky note on guitar because there's no open F string. Knowing where F lives on each string is essential for playing in the key of F major or for finding F-rooted chords. The 8th fret of the A string is also where you'd root an F power chord or F barre chord in the A-string barre shape.

G on the tenth fret. This is the same note as the open G string (3rd string) — one octave lower. This equivalence is useful for checking your fretboard knowledge: 10th fret of A should sound like the open G string. When playing bass lines, this G position connects smoothly to notes on the D string above.

A on the twelfth fret — full circle, one octave above the open A string. The 12th fret always equals the open string plus one octave, on every string. This is marked with double dots on the fretboard. Everything above fret 12 repeats the 0-11 pattern. If you know the first 12 frets of the A string, you know all of them.

description: Fretboard note quiz — name the note at each position on the E and A strings.
action: Test yourself: for each fret position, name the note without looking at a reference. Then check your answer.

description: Learn the chromatic scale — all 12 notes in order.
action: Play every fret from 0 to 12 on the low E string. Say each note name: E, F, F#, G, G#, A, A#, B, C, C#, D, D#, E. This is the chromatic scale — all 12 notes.
context: The chromatic scale contains every note in Western music. Understanding it means understanding the building blocks of every chord and scale.
constraint_note: Every fret is a half step. Two frets = whole step. The pattern repeats at fret 12 (one octave higher). Sharps (#) live between natural notes, except between B-C and E-F.

items.body: Open E — the lowest standard note on guitar. Home base for the low E string. Many iconic riffs start here. Learn to hear this pitch and you can always orient yourself on the fretboard.
F — one half step up. The E-F half step is one of only two natural half steps in music (the other is B-C). No sharp or flat between them. This is why the F chord sits at fret 1.
F#/Gb — the first sharp note. F# is the same pitch as Gb; which name you use depends on the key. In the key of G major, it's F#. In the key of Db major, it's Gb. Same fret, same sound, two names.
G — root of G major, one of the most common keys. The 3rd fret is where G power chords start. The E-to-G distance (3 frets = minor third) gives blues its characteristic dark sound.

G#/Ab — another enharmonic pair. G# appears in the key of A major and E major. Ab appears in keys with flats like Eb major. On guitar you don't need to worry about the name — just know the sound and position.
A — crucial reference point. This should sound exactly like the open A string. The 5th-fret tuning method starts here. A is the universal tuning reference (A440). Power chords rooted here are A5.
A#/Bb — the note between A and B. Bb is extremely common in jazz and R&B. On the low E string, this position is useful for Bb power chords and for walking bass lines that pass through Bb.

B — the fifth of E. Playing E and B together sounds powerful and stable — that's the root-fifth relationship behind every power chord. B is also the second natural half step destination: B to C (fret 7 to 8) has no sharp between them.

C — the 'home base' of music theory. C major has no sharps or flats. On the low E string, C at fret 8 is useful for walking bass lines. The B-to-C half step (fret 7 to 8) is the second natural half step.
C#/Db — common in keys like A major (C#) and Db major (Db). Getting into higher fret territory now. The frets are closer together up here, which makes fingering easier but reading position harder.
D — same note as the open D string, one octave lower. This equivalence helps verify your fretboard knowledge. D at fret 10 is where D-rooted power chords live in this position.

D#/Eb — the last note before the octave. Eb is common in jazz, blues, and brass-heavy music. On the low E string, this position is rarely used for chord roots but appears in chromatic runs and bass lines.
E — full circle, one octave above open E. The 12th fret always equals the open string one octave higher. Double dots mark this on most guitars. Everything above fret 12 repeats the 0-11 pattern.

description: Learn the E minor pentatonic scale — box 1 (the most important scale shape in guitar).
action: Learn the Em pentatonic box 1 pattern. Play it ascending and descending 5 times. This is the scale behind virtually all rock and blues solos.
context: The minor pentatonic is the #1 scale for improvisation. Box 1 at the open position (Em) is where most guitarists start their soloing journey.
constraint_note: Use alternate picking. Each note should ring clearly. Play slowly enough that every note is clean. Speed comes after accuracy.

description: Learn the A minor pentatonic scale — box 1 at fret 5 (the most common solo position).
action: Learn Am pentatonic box 1: same shape as Em pentatonic but starting at fret 5. Play ascending and descending 5 times.
context: Fret 5 is the most popular position for soloing because it's comfortable and central on the neck. Countless classic solos live in this box.
constraint_note: This is the SAME shape as Em pentatonic, just moved to fret 5. If you know the open position shape, you already know this one.

description: Learn the C major scale in open position.
action: Play the C major scale in open position: C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C. Start on A string fret 3 (C) and play up to high E string fret 0 (E). Ascending and descending.
context: The C major scale has no sharps or flats — it's the 'white keys' of the piano. Learning it on guitar teaches you the major scale pattern that applies to every key.
constraint_note: Pay attention to the whole-step / half-step pattern: W-W-H-W-W-W-H. This pattern defines every major scale. The half steps are between E-F and B-C.

description: Fretboard note quiz — all strings, natural notes only.
action: Name the note at each position. No sharps/flats — only natural notes (A B C D E F G).

items.prompt: D string, fret 5: ___
items.hint: Same as the open G string

items.prompt: G string, fret 5: ___
items.hint: Same as the open... wait, no open C string. Think: G + 5 frets

items.prompt: B string, fret 1: ___
items.hint: B to C is always 1 fret

items.prompt: G string, fret 2: ___
items.hint: G + whole step

items.prompt: D string, fret 3: ___
items.hint: D + 3 frets, remember E-F is 1 fret

items.prompt: B string, fret 3: ___
items.hint: B + 3 frets

description: Learn the G major scale — two octaves in open position.
action: Play the G major scale starting from low E fret 3 (G). Notes: G-A-B-C-D-E-F#-G. Play two octaves ascending and descending.
context: G major is one of the most guitar-friendly keys. Many open chords (G, C, D, Em, Am) belong to this key. Knowing the scale connects your chords to melody.
constraint_note: F# is the only sharp in G major. It's on the low E string fret 2, D string fret 4, and high E string fret 2. Everything else is natural notes.

description: Learn pentatonic box 2 — expand your soloing range.
action: Learn Am pentatonic box 2 (starts at fret 8). This connects directly above box 1. Practice ascending and descending, then try connecting box 1 → box 2 seamlessly.
context: Box 1 alone limits you to 4 frets. Adding box 2 doubles your range and lets you move up the neck during solos. The connection point is where the magic happens.
constraint_note: The top notes of box 1 overlap with the bottom notes of box 2. Find those shared notes — they're your bridge between positions.

description: Scale sequence exercise — play the pentatonic in groups of 3.
action: Play the Am pentatonic (box 1) in groups of 3 ascending: notes 1-2-3, then 2-3-4, then 3-4-5, etc. This creates a musical pattern instead of just running up and down.
context: Sequences make scales sound musical instead of like exercises. Playing in groups of 3 or 4 is how real solos are built — not by running scales linearly.
constraint_note: Keep the rhythm even — each group of 3 should take the same amount of time. Use alternate picking throughout.

description: Octave shapes — find the same note in different positions on the fretboard.
action: Learn the octave shapes: from any note on the E string, the same note is 2 frets up and 2 strings over (on D string). From A string, same rule to G string. Play A on E string fret 5, then find A on D string fret 7. Continue for G, C, D.
context: Octave shapes are the fastest way to navigate the fretboard. Once you know one note's position, octave shapes instantly show you where it lives on other strings.
constraint_note: The rule: same note = 2 frets higher on the string 2 positions thinner (skip one string). Exception: G to B string adds 1 extra fret due to tuning.

items.primary: E string fret 5 → D string fret 7
items.body: From any note on the low E string, the same note one octave higher is 2 frets up and 2 strings over (on the D string). Example: E at fret 0 → E at fret 2 on D string. This shape is incredibly useful for finding notes quickly. Practice this shape until it's automatic — it's one of the most practical fretboard navigation tools.

items.primary: A string fret 3 → G string fret 5
items.body: Same shape works from the A string to the G string: 2 frets up, 2 strings over. Playing a note and its octave together creates a full, powerful sound. The Red Hot Chili Peppers' bassist Flea uses octave shapes constantly. This is also how you verify you've found the right note — if the octave sounds 'the same but higher,' you're correct.

items.primary: E string fret 3 → D string fret 5
items.body: Another example of the 2-frets-up, 2-strings-over pattern. G at fret 3 on E string → G at fret 5 on D string. Notice fret 5 on D is also the open G string — three ways to play the same note! The fretboard is full of these equivalences. Learning octave shapes helps you see them and choose the most convenient position for any musical context.

items.primary: D string fret 5 → B string fret 8
items.reveal: Both are G (extra fret for B string)
items.body: Here's the exception: because of the G-to-B tuning irregularity, this octave shape is 3 frets up and 2 strings over (not 2 frets like before). Any shape that crosses the G-B string boundary shifts up by one fret. This catches many guitarists off guard. Memorize it: E/A string octaves = 2 frets up; when crossing G-B = 3 frets up.

description: Learn the natural minor scale pattern and compare it to the major scale.
action: Play A natural minor: A-B-C-D-E-F-G-A (same notes as C major but starting on A). Compare the sound to A major: A-B-C#-D-E-F#-G#-A. The difference is 3 notes: C vs C#, F vs F#, G vs G#.
context: Major and minor scales share the same pattern — they just start on different notes. A minor = C major starting on A. This relationship (relative major/minor) is fundamental to music theory.
constraint_note: Play A major then A minor back to back. The 3 flattened notes (3rd, 6th, 7th) are what create the 'sad' minor sound vs the 'happy' major sound.

description: Connect all 5 pentatonic boxes across the full neck.
action: Play the Am pentatonic through all 5 box positions: box 1 (fret 5) → box 2 (fret 8) → box 3 (fret 10) → box 4 (fret 12) → box 5 (fret 3/15). Ascend through all boxes, then descend.
context: Connecting all 5 boxes gives you access to the entire fretboard for soloing. Most guitarists get stuck in box 1 — breaking out transforms your lead playing.
constraint_note: The transition between boxes is the hard part. Find the shared notes between adjacent boxes and use them as pivot points. Go slowly at the transitions.

description: Learn the Dorian mode — the 'jazzy minor' scale.
action: Play A Dorian: A-B-C-D-E-F#-G-A. It's like A natural minor but with a raised 6th (F# instead of F). Compare the sound to A natural minor.
context: Dorian is the most commonly used mode after major and minor. It has a sophisticated, jazzy quality — think Santana, Miles Davis, or Daft Punk's 'Get Lucky'.
constraint_note: The only difference from natural minor is one note: the 6th is raised. Play A minor then A Dorian — hear how that one note changes the entire mood from dark to sophisticated.

description: Learn the Mixolydian mode — the 'bluesy major' scale.
action: Play A Mixolydian: A-B-C#-D-E-F#-G-A. It's like A major but with a flatted 7th (G instead of G#). This is the sound of blues-rock and classic rock.
context: Mixolydian is the scale behind AC/DC, Lynyrd Skynyrd, and most blues-rock. The flatted 7th gives it a bluesy edge that pure major lacks.
constraint_note: Compare A major (G#) to A Mixolydian (G natural). That one note is the difference between 'pop' and 'rock'. Play both and hear it.

description: Fretboard speed drill — name notes as fast as possible on a random string.
action: Pick a random string. Starting from the open note, name every natural note ascending to fret 12 as fast as you can. Time yourself. Then try descending. Goal: under 10 seconds per string.
context: Fast note recall is what lets you find chord roots, scale positions, and navigate the neck in real-time during playing. It should be automatic, not calculated.
constraint_note: Only natural notes (no sharps/flats). Skip the frets with sharps. E string: E-F-G-A-B-C-D-E. Time yourself and try to beat your record.

description: Interval shapes on the fretboard — learn to see intervals visually.
action: Learn these interval shapes from any root note: minor 3rd = 3 frets up same string (or 1 string over, 2 frets back). Perfect 5th = 2 frets up, 1 string over. Octave = 2 frets up, 2 strings over. Practice finding each from different starting notes.
context: Seeing intervals as shapes on the fretboard lets you build any chord or scale from any position. It's the key to truly understanding the instrument, not just memorizing patterns.
constraint_note: Start with root on the E string. Find the minor 3rd, major 3rd, perfect 5th, and octave. Then move the root to the A string and find them again.

items.body: A minor third is 3 frets up from any note on the same string. It's the interval that makes minor chords sound sad or dark. Play any note, then the note 3 frets higher — that melancholy sound is a minor third. The opening of 'Greensleeves' or 'Hey Jude' (the 'Jude' leap) uses a minor third. On the fretboard, minor thirds are easy to see: just count 3 frets.

A major third is 4 frets up — one fret more than a minor third. This single fret difference is what separates happy from sad in music. Play a note and go up 4 frets: that bright, cheerful sound is a major third. The opening of 'When the Saints Go Marching In' starts with a major third. The difference between a major and minor chord is literally this one fret — the third is either 3 or 4 frets from the root.

items.reveal: Same fret on the string 2 positions thinner (for E/A strings)
items.body: A perfect fifth is 7 frets up — or equivalently, 2 frets up on the next lower string. This is the most consonant interval after the octave, and it's the basis of power chords. Play any note and the note 7 frets higher: that strong, stable, 'powerful' sound is a fifth. Power chords (used in rock, punk, metal) are just root + fifth. The fifth is so stable that it works over almost any chord — it's the safest note to play when you're unsure.

An octave is 12 frets — the same note, one register higher. The opening of "Somewhere Over the Rainbow" is an octave leap. Your brain perceives octaves as the same pitch at different heights, which is why the 12th fret has double dots: the pattern repeats. Octave shapes on guitar (2 frets up + 2 strings over, or 3 frets when crossing G-B) are essential navigation tools.

description: Scale improvisation — solo over a backing track using the Am pentatonic.
action: Play random notes from the Am pentatonic box 1 over a simple Am chord (strum Am, then solo, then strum again). Focus on making musical phrases, not just running the scale up and down.
context: The goal of learning scales is improvisation. This exercise bridges the gap between 'knowing the scale' and 'making music with it'. Even random notes from the pentatonic sound good.
constraint_note: Try these techniques: repeat a note, bend a note, leave silence between phrases, end phrases on the root (A). Music is about rhythm and space, not just notes.

description: Learn to count beats: tap your foot on 1-2-3-4 in 4/4 time.
action: Set the metronome to 80 BPM. Tap your foot on every beat. Count aloud: 1-2-3-4, 1-2-3-4. Do this for 2 minutes until the beat feels natural in your body.
context: Rhythm starts in your body, not your hands. If your foot can't keep time, your strumming won't either. This is the foundation everything else builds on.
constraint_note: Your foot tap should land EXACTLY on the metronome click. Not before, not after. If you drift, slow down. Precision matters more than speed.

description: Practice all-downstroke strumming on a single chord.
action: Hold an Em chord. Strum DOWN on every beat (1-2-3-4) with the metronome. Keep your arm moving in a steady pendulum motion. Do this for 2 minutes without stopping.
context: All downstrokes is the simplest strumming pattern and the basis of punk rock, early rock and roll, and many folk songs. Master this before adding upstrokes.
constraint_note: Your strumming arm should move like a pendulum — steady and even. Don't strum from the elbow; use your wrist. Every strum should sound the same volume.

description: Learn the down-up strumming pattern: D U D U D U D U.
action: Hold a G chord. Strum DOWN on the beats (1-2-3-4) and UP on the 'ands' (1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and). Your arm moves continuously — down on beats, up between beats.
context: Down-up is the fundamental strumming motion. Your arm should ALWAYS be moving — even when you skip a strum later, the arm keeps the pendulum going.
constraint_note: Count aloud: '1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and'. Downs are louder (full strum), ups are lighter (just the top 3-4 strings). Keep the arm moving constantly.

description: Practice the most common strumming pattern: D - D U - U D U.
action: Learn this pattern: D - D U - U D U (down, miss, down up, miss, up down up). Your arm keeps the pendulum going on the misses — it swings but doesn't hit the strings.
context: This single pattern works for hundreds of songs — from pop to folk to rock. It's the 'universal' strumming pattern that every guitarist should have in muscle memory.
constraint_note: The 'misses' are crucial — your arm still moves, it just doesn't touch the strings. This keeps the rhythm steady. Count: 1 (D) and (miss) 2 (D) and (U) 3 (miss) and (U) 4 (D) and (U).

reference.body:
Beat:  1   &   2   &   3   &   4   &
Strum: D   -   D   U   -   U   D   U
       ↓       ↓   ↑       ↑   ↓   ↑

description: Clapping rhythm exercise — internalize rhythms without a guitar.
action: Clap these rhythms with the metronome: (1) Quarter notes: clap on every beat. (2) Eighth notes: clap twice per beat. (3) The 'D-DU-UDU' pattern: clap the strumming pattern. (4) Syncopation: clap on the 'ands' only.
context: Rhythm is a body skill, not a hand skill. Clapping rhythms away from the guitar builds internal timing that transfers directly to strumming.
constraint_note: Each rhythm for 1 minute. The syncopation pattern (clapping on 'ands') will feel weird — that's the point. It trains you to feel the offbeats.

description: Practice accented strumming — emphasize beats 2 and 4.
action: Strum D U D U D U D U continuously on any chord. Make beats 2 and 4 LOUDER than beats 1 and 3. This creates a natural groove that makes your playing sound musical.
context: In most popular music, beats 2 and 4 are the 'backbeat' — where the snare drum hits. Accenting these beats on guitar makes your strumming groove instead of plod.
constraint_note: Beats 1 and 3 should be noticeably softer. Beats 2 and 4 should pop. This is subtle but transforms your sound from 'beginner' to 'musical'.

description: Practice palm muting — the chunky rhythm guitar sound.
action: Rest the edge of your picking hand lightly on the strings near the bridge. Strum — the sound should be muted and chunky, not dead. Practice alternating between open strums and palm-muted strums.
context: Palm muting is essential for rock, metal, and dynamic playing. It creates the 'chug' sound and lets you control dynamics — loud open strums vs quiet muted strums.
constraint_note: Hand position is everything: too far from the bridge = dead thud. Too close = no muting. Find the sweet spot where strings still ring but sound chunky.

description: Learn the folk strumming pattern: D - D U D U.
action: Learn this pattern: D - D U D U (down, miss, down up, down up). Practice on G-C-D progression, one bar per chord.
context: This pattern is the backbone of folk, country, and acoustic pop. It's simpler than the universal pattern and has a driving, forward-moving feel.
constraint_note: The miss on beat 'and-1' gives the pattern its characteristic feel. Keep the arm pendulum going through the miss.

reference.body:
Beat:  1   &   2   &   3   &   4   &
Strum: D   -   D   U   D   U   D   U
       ↓       ↓   ↑   ↓   ↑   ↓   ↑

description: Practice the reggae/ska upstroke pattern.
action: Strum ONLY on the upstrokes (the 'ands'): - U - U - U - U. Mute the strings briefly between each upstroke for the classic reggae chop. Practice on Am and Dm.
context: Reggae guitar is all upstrokes on the offbeats. It sounds simple but requires excellent timing and muting control. It's a great exercise for rhythmic independence.
constraint_note: The downbeat should be silent (muted). Only the upstroke rings. This feels backwards at first. Keep your foot tapping on the downbeats while your hand strums on the upbeats.

reference.body:
Beat:  1   &   2   &   3   &   4   &
Strum: -   U   -   U   -   U   -   U
           ↑       ↑       ↑       ↑

description: Practice 16th note strumming — double the speed of eighth notes.
action: Strum 16th notes: 4 strums per beat (D U D U on each beat). Count: 1-e-and-a, 2-e-and-a, 3-e-and-a, 4-e-and-a. Start at a slow tempo.
context: 16th notes are the basis of funk, R&B, and fast strumming patterns. They require a faster arm motion and more precise control.
constraint_note: Start at 60 BPM (which means 4 strums per second at that tempo). Keep the volume even. Your wrist does the work, not your arm.

description: Practice syncopated strumming — accent the offbeats for a funky feel.
action: Play this syncopated pattern: D - - U - U D - (down, miss, miss, up, miss, up, down, miss). The accents fall on unexpected beats, creating a funky groove.
context: Syncopation is what makes rhythm interesting. It's the difference between a marching band (on the beat) and a funk band (around the beat). It creates tension and groove.
constraint_note: The arm pendulum NEVER stops. Misses are just swings that don't touch strings. The pattern feels 'wrong' at first — that's syncopation working.

reference.body:
Beat:  1   &   2   &   3   &   4   &
Strum: D   -   -   U   -   U   D   -
       ↓               ↑       ↑   ↓

description: Practice muted strums (ghost strums) mixed with open strums.
action: Strum D U D U continuously. On beats 2 and 4, release fretting pressure so the strum produces a percussive 'chk' sound instead of a chord. This creates a built-in drum pattern.
context: Ghost strums add percussion to your playing. Professional acoustic guitarists use them constantly — it's what makes solo guitar sound full and rhythmic.
constraint_note: The muted strum should sound like a snare hit — a sharp 'chk'. Release fretting pressure but keep fingers touching the strings. Don't lift them off completely.

description: Practice strumming pattern changes — switch patterns mid-song.
action: Play 4 bars of all-downstrokes, then switch to 4 bars of D-DU-UDU, then 4 bars of the folk pattern. The chord stays the same (G) — only the strumming changes. Transition should be seamless.
context: Real songs change strumming patterns between sections (verse vs chorus). Being able to switch patterns without losing the beat is an essential performance skill.
constraint_note: The metronome beat should never waver during the pattern switch. If you lose the beat during transitions, slow down until the switch is seamless.

description: Practice playing in 3/4 time (waltz time).
action: Set the metronome to 3/4 time (or count in groups of 3). Strum: D - U D - U (1-2-3, 1-2-3). Accent beat 1. Play on Am-E-Am-E progression.
context: 3/4 time is used in waltzes, many ballads, and songs like Nothing Else Matters (Metallica) and Norwegian Wood (Beatles). It has a swaying, circular feel.
constraint_note: Count 1-2-3, 1-2-3 instead of 1-2-3-4. Beat 1 gets the accent. It should feel like a waltz — ONE-two-three, ONE-two-three.

reference.body:
Beat:  1   2   3   1   2   3
Strum: D   -   U   D   -   U
       ↓       ↑   ↓       ↑

description: Tempo training — play the same pattern at progressively faster tempos.
action: Play the D-DU-UDU pattern on G chord. Start at 60 BPM for 1 minute. Increase by 10 BPM each minute. When you lose the groove, drop back 10 BPM and hold there.
context: Tempo control is what separates a rhythm guitarist from someone who just strums. Being able to play cleanly at any tempo — slow or fast — is the mark of a solid player.
constraint_note: 'Losing the groove' means: strums become uneven, chord changes are late, or your foot stops tapping in time. Any of these = too fast.

description: Practice the shuffle rhythm — the swing feel used in blues and jazz.
action: Instead of even eighth notes (straight feel), play with a shuffle: the downbeat is long, the upbeat is short. Think 'da-DUM da-DUM' instead of 'da-da da-da'. Play on E-A-B7 blues progression.
context: The shuffle is the heartbeat of blues. It's a triplet feel where the first two notes of each triplet are tied together. Once you hear it, you'll recognize it everywhere.
constraint_note: Think of the rhythm as 'long-short, long-short'. The down strum lasts about 2/3 of the beat, the up strum gets the remaining 1/3. It should swing, not march.

description: Practice the boom-chick pattern — alternating bass note and chord strum.
action: On a G chord: pick the bass note (low E fret 3) on beat 1, strum the upper strings on beat 2, pick the alternate bass (A fret 2) on beat 3, strum on beat 4. This is the boom-chick pattern.
context: Boom-chick is the foundation of country, folk, and fingerstyle accompaniment. It creates the illusion of a bass player and rhythm guitarist playing together.
constraint_note: The bass note (boom) should be a single picked note, not a strum. The chord (chick) should be the upper 3-4 strings only. Alternate between root and 5th for the bass.

description: Rhythm reading exercise — play rhythms from notation.
action: Read and play these rhythm patterns on a muted string (no chord, just rhythm). Pattern 1: quarter-quarter-half. Pattern 2: eighth-eighth-quarter-half. Pattern 3: dotted quarter-eighth-half.
context: Reading rhythms is a transferable skill that helps you learn songs from sheet music, communicate with other musicians, and understand song structure.
constraint_note: A quarter note = 1 beat. Half note = 2 beats. Eighth note = half a beat. Dotted quarter = 1.5 beats. Count aloud while playing.

steps: Pattern 1: strum-strum-struuuum (1, 2, 3-4)
Pattern 2: strum-strum-strum-struuuum (1-and, 2, 3-4)
Pattern 3: struuum-strum-struuuum (1-2-and, 3-4)
Play each pattern 8 times, then move to the next

description: Practice dynamic strumming — play soft verses and loud choruses.
action: Play G-Em-C-D progression. First 4 bars: strum very softly (pianissimo). Next 4 bars: medium volume. Next 4 bars: full volume (fortissimo). Then back to soft. Control the volume with strum intensity.
context: Dynamics — the contrast between loud and soft — is what makes music emotional. A song that's the same volume throughout is boring. Dynamics create drama.
constraint_note: The chord voicings and pattern stay the same. ONLY the volume changes. Soft strumming should still be rhythmically precise. Don't sacrifice timing for dynamics.

description: Polyrhythm introduction — tap 3 against 4.
action: Tap your right hand in groups of 4 (1-2-3-4) while tapping your left hand in groups of 3 (1-2-3). They should align on beat 1 of every 12 taps. Use the phrase 'pass the bread and butter' to feel the rhythm.
context: Polyrhythms appear in African music, jazz, and progressive rock. Even a basic understanding of 3-against-4 opens your ears to complex rhythmic textures.
constraint_note: This is HARD. Start by tapping each hand separately, then combine. The mnemonic 'pass the BREAD and BUT-ter' places the 3-beat accents within the 4-beat framework.

description: Learn right-hand finger names and positions for fingerpicking: p-i-m-a.
action: Learn the right-hand finger assignments: p (thumb) = bass strings E-A-D, i (index) = G string, m (middle) = B string, a (ring) = high E string. Place your hand in position and pluck each string with its assigned finger.
context: Fingerpicking assigns each right-hand finger to specific strings. This fixed assignment is the foundation — once it's automatic, complex patterns become possible.
constraint_note: Your thumb handles the 3 bass strings. Each finger gets one treble string. Your pinky rests on the guitar body for stability. Don't move your hand — only the fingers move.

items.body: The thumb handles the three bass strings: E, A, and D. It moves differently from the fingers — pushing downward and away from the palm. The thumb provides the bass line and rhythm foundation in fingerpicking. Common mistake: letting the thumb collapse inward instead of staying extended. In classical technique, the thumb should be slightly ahead of the fingers, creating a stable 'shelf.'

The index finger is assigned to the G string (3rd string). It's your most controlled finger and often carries the melody. The motion should come from the last knuckle — curl the fingertip toward the palm to pluck. Avoid pulling the string away from the guitar body; instead, push through and rest on the next string. This 'rest stroke' produces a fuller tone.

The middle finger handles the B string (2nd string). It's typically your strongest finger and alternates with the index for fast passages. The i-m alternation is the foundation of classical guitar speed technique. Many fingerpicking patterns use a 'pinch' where thumb and middle finger play simultaneously, creating a bass-melody combination.

The ring finger is assigned to the high E string (1st string). It's the weakest picking finger and needs the most practice. A common exercise: hold a chord and play p-i-m-a in sequence, making each note equally loud. The ring finger tends to move with the middle finger — developing independence between 'm' and 'a' is one of fingerpicking's biggest challenges.

description: Basic p-i-m-a arpeggio on an Am chord.
action: Hold an Am chord. Play: p (A string) → i (G string) → m (B string) → a (high E string). One note at a time, evenly spaced. Repeat this pattern continuously for 2 minutes.
context: The p-i-m-a arpeggio is the 'Hello World' of fingerpicking. It turns any chord into a flowing, harp-like sound. This single pattern works with every chord you know.
constraint_note: Each note should ring into the next — don't mute previous notes. The sound should build up like a harp. Keep the tempo steady and even.

description: Practice p-i-m-a on different chords: Am, C, G, Em.
action: Play the p-i-m-a arpeggio pattern on each chord for 1 minute: Am (thumb on A string), C (thumb on A string), G (thumb on E string), Em (thumb on E string). Switch chords without breaking the pattern.
context: The same fingerpicking pattern sounds completely different on each chord. Learning to maintain the pattern while changing chords is the core fingerpicking skill.
constraint_note: When the chord changes, your left hand moves but your right hand keeps the same pattern. The thumb string may change (A string for Am/C, E string for G/Em).

description: Practice the reverse arpeggio: a-m-i-p (descending).
action: Hold a C chord. Play: a (high E) → m (B) → i (G) → p (A). This descending pattern creates a different mood — more melancholic and introspective. Practice on C, Am, Em.
context: Ascending arpeggios (p-i-m-a) sound hopeful and building. Descending arpeggios (a-m-i-p) sound reflective and falling. Using both gives you emotional range.
constraint_note: The descending pattern is harder because your ring finger (a) leads. It's the weakest finger. Focus on making the first note (a) as strong as the others.

description: Practice the pinch technique — thumb and finger pluck simultaneously.
action: Hold an Em chord. Pinch: play the bass note (p on E string) and a treble note (m on B string) at the exact same time. Then alternate: pinch, then i alone, then pinch, then a alone.
context: The pinch creates a full, rich sound — bass and treble together. It's used to start phrases, accent beats, and create the 'boom' in boom-chick fingerpicking patterns.
constraint_note: The thumb and finger must hit at EXACTLY the same time. If one is early, it sounds like two separate notes instead of one rich chord. Practice the synchronization.

description: Practice thumb independence — alternating bass while fingers hold steady.
action: Hold a G chord. Alternate the thumb between E string (fret 3) and A string (fret 2) in steady quarter notes. While the thumb alternates, keep your i-m-a fingers still. Just the thumb moves.
context: Thumb independence is the gateway to Travis picking. Your thumb becomes an autonomous bass player while your fingers handle melody. This separation is the hardest part.
constraint_note: ONLY the thumb moves. Your other fingers stay planted on their assigned strings. The thumb should be completely steady — like a metronome itself.

description: Practice the inside-outside fingerpicking pattern.
action: Hold an Am chord. Play: p (A) → i (G) → a (high E) → m (B). This 'inside-outside' pattern (bass, inner treble, outer treble, middle treble) creates a rolling, wave-like sound.
context: The inside-outside pattern is one of the most musical fingerpicking patterns. It appears in classical guitar, folk, and pop ballads. It sounds more complex than it is.
constraint_note: The pattern crosses over itself (i then a then m). This feels unnatural at first because your fingers want to go in order. Practice slowly until the crossing is smooth.

description: Learn basic Travis picking — alternating bass with simple melody.
action: Hold a C chord. Thumb alternates: C (A fret 3) on beat 1, E (D fret 2) on beat 2, C on beat 3, E on beat 4. Now add index finger: pluck G string between beats 2 and 3. That's basic Travis picking.
context: Travis picking (named after Merle Travis) creates the illusion of two guitarists — a bass player and a melody player. It's the foundation of fingerstyle guitar.
constraint_note: The thumb NEVER stops alternating. It's the heartbeat. The melody notes (index finger) are added between the bass notes. If the thumb falters, stop the melody and fix the bass first.

description: Travis picking on G chord with melody notes.
