action: Hold G chord. Thumb alternates: G (E fret 3) and B (A fret 2). Add melody: m plucks B string between beats 1-2, i plucks G string between beats 3-4. Practice until the bass is steady and melody floats on top.
context: G is the most natural key for Travis picking because the bass notes are on the two thickest strings. Many classic fingerstyle songs are in G.
constraint_note: The bass must be rock-solid before adding melody. If adding melody disrupts the bass, go back to bass-only for 1 minute, then try again.

description: Practice fingerpicking with chord changes: Am → C → G → Em.
action: Play the p-i-m-a arpeggio pattern. Change chords every bar (4 beats). The fingerpicking pattern continues unbroken through the chord changes. 2 minutes per loop.
context: Maintaining a fingerpicking pattern through chord changes is the real skill. Your right hand must be on autopilot so your brain can focus on the left-hand chord changes.
constraint_note: The pattern should sound continuous — no gaps or hesitations at chord changes. If there's a gap, the chord change is too slow. Practice the changes separately first.

description: Practice the classical arpeggio pattern: p-i-m-a-m-i.
action: Hold an Am chord. Play: p-i-m-a-m-i (up then back down). This creates a flowing, classical sound. Each cycle = 6 notes. Practice on Am, E, Dm.
context: The ascending-descending arpeggio is the signature sound of classical guitar. It appears in pieces like Romanza and Stairway to Heaven's intro.
constraint_note: The turnaround (a→m) should be seamless — no pause at the top. The pattern should sound like one continuous wave, not two separate directions.

description: Fingerpicking speed builder — gradually increase arpeggio tempo.
action: Play p-i-m-a on Am. Start at 50 BPM, 1 minute. Increase by 5 BPM each minute. When notes start blurring together or fingers stumble, drop back 5 BPM.
context: Fingerpicking speed comes from relaxation, not tension. If your hand tenses up at higher speeds, you've gone too fast. Speed is a byproduct of clean, relaxed technique.
constraint_note: Watch for tension in your right hand. At the first sign of tightness, slow down. Shake out your hand. Speed with tension leads to injury.

description: Practice Travis picking with a walking bass line.
action: Play a G-C progression. On G: thumb walks G(E3) → A(E5) → B(A2) → C(A3), arriving at the C chord root. While the thumb walks, fingers maintain a simple melody on treble strings.
context: Walking bass lines connect chords smoothly — the bass 'walks' from one chord root to the next instead of jumping. It's what makes fingerstyle arrangements sound professional.
constraint_note: The walking bass notes should be evenly spaced and lead naturally to the next chord's root note. The melody continues independently above the walking bass.

description: Practice simultaneous bass and melody — the core of solo fingerstyle.
action: Hold a C chord. Thumb plays a steady bass (A string, quarter notes). Simultaneously, play a simple melody on the high E string with your ring finger: fret 0, 1, 3, 1, 0. Bass and melody are independent.
context: Playing bass and melody simultaneously is what makes solo guitar sound like a complete arrangement. It's the ultimate expression of finger independence.
constraint_note: The bass and melody have different rhythms. The bass is steady quarter notes. The melody moves at its own pace. Your brain must track both independently.

description: Practice fingerpicking dynamics — soft and loud within the same pattern.
action: Play p-i-m-a on Em. Make the thumb (bass) loud and the fingers (treble) soft. Then reverse: soft bass, loud treble. Then try: loud on beats 1 and 3, soft on 2 and 4.
context: Dynamic control in fingerpicking is what makes the difference between mechanical playing and musical playing. It's how you create emphasis, mood, and expression.
constraint_note: Controlling individual finger volume is advanced. Start with just thumb-loud/fingers-soft. The volume difference should be obvious, not subtle.

description: Learn a complete fingerpicking arrangement of a simple melody.
action: Play this simple arrangement of 'Twinkle Twinkle Little Star' in C: bass notes on beats 1 and 3 (thumb), melody notes on beats 2 and 4 (fingers). The melody: C-C-G-G-A-A-G (frets: 1-1-0-0-0-0-0 on B string, with open G and E strings).
context: Arranging a melody for solo guitar is the ultimate fingerpicking skill. Starting with a simple melody you already know lets you focus on the technique, not the notes.
constraint_note: The melody should be clearly audible above the bass. If the bass drowns out the melody, play the bass softer. The listener should hear the tune.

description: Practice the flamenco rasgueado technique — rapid finger strumming.
action: Curl your right-hand fingers into a loose fist. Flick them open one at a time across the strings: pinky, ring, middle, index — in rapid succession. This creates a fast, rolling strum. Practice on Am.
context: Rasgueado is a flamenco technique that creates a machine-gun strum effect. Even a basic version adds dramatic flair to your playing.
constraint_note: Each finger should produce a distinct strum — not one blurred sound. Start slowly: flick each finger with a clear gap between them. Speed up only when each strum is distinct.

description: Learn to identify major vs minor chords by ear.
action: Play these chord pairs and listen to the difference: E major then E minor. A major then A minor. D major then D minor. Major sounds 'happy/bright'. Minor sounds 'sad/dark'. Play each pair 5 times.
context: Distinguishing major from minor is the most fundamental ear training skill. Once you can hear this difference, you can identify the mood of any song instantly.
constraint_note: Close your eyes after playing each chord. Let the sound wash over you. Major chords feel resolved and bright. Minor chords feel tense and melancholic. The difference is just one note.

description: Learn interval sounds using song associations.
action: Learn to recognize intervals by associating them with famous song openings. Play each interval on the guitar and sing the song reference.
context: Interval recognition is the foundation of playing by ear. Once you can identify intervals, you can figure out any melody just by listening.

items.body: The minor second — just one fret — is the most dissonant common interval. It's the 'Jaws' theme: that creeping, menacing half-step. It's also the first two notes of 'Für Elise' by Beethoven. On guitar, play any fret then the next fret up — that tense, clashing sound is a minor second. In horror movie scores, minor seconds are everywhere because they trigger an instinctive unease.

Two frets apart — a whole step. This is the interval between most adjacent notes in a major scale (do-re, re-mi, fa-sol, etc.). The first two notes of 'Happy Birthday' form a major second. It sounds neutral and stepwise — neither happy nor sad, just movement. On guitar, two frets is the most common distance you'll move when playing scales.

Three frets — the sad interval. The opening of 'Greensleeves,' the 'Hey' to 'Jude' in Hey Jude, or the NBC chime (first two notes). Minor thirds are the building blocks of minor chords and give blues its characteristic melancholy. When you hear a melody that tugs at your emotions, there's often a minor third lurking in it.

Four frets — the happy interval. The first two notes of 'When the Saints Go Marching In' or 'Kumbaya.' Major thirds make major chords sound bright and cheerful. The difference between major and minor — between happy and sad in music — is literally one fret: 3 frets (minor) vs 4 frets (major). Train your ear to hear this difference and you'll understand harmony intuitively.

Five frets — the 'Here Comes the Bride' interval. Also the opening of 'Amazing Grace.' The perfect fourth has a strong, open quality. On guitar, it's special because it's the tuning interval between most adjacent strings. When you tune your guitar using the 5th-fret method, you're matching perfect fourths. This interval appears in countless melodies and is one of the easiest to recognize by ear.

Seven frets — the 'Star Wars' theme opening, or 'Twinkle Twinkle Little Star.' The perfect fifth is the most consonant interval after the octave — it sounds stable, powerful, and complete. Power chords are built on fifths. The fifth is so fundamental that it appears in virtually every musical tradition worldwide. If you can only recognize one interval by ear, make it the fifth.

Twelve frets — the same note, higher. The opening of 'Somewhere Over the Rainbow' is an octave leap. An octave sounds like the 'same note but different' — your brain perceives octaves as equivalent pitches. This is why the 12th fret has double dots: it's where the fretboard pattern repeats. Training your ear to recognize octaves is the foundation of all interval recognition — once you can hear 'same note, different height,' other intervals become easier to distinguish.

description: Tune your guitar by ear using the 5th fret method.
action: Tune your guitar using relative tuning: play the 5th fret of the low E string — it should match the open A string. 5th fret of A = open D. 5th fret of D = open G. 4th fret of G = open B. 5th fret of B = open high E.
context: Tuning by ear trains your pitch recognition and ensures you can always tune your guitar, even without a tuner. It's a practical ear training exercise you'll use for life.
constraint_note: The two notes should sound identical. If one is higher, tune it down. If lower, tune up. Listen for 'beats' (a wobbling sound) — when the beats disappear, the strings are in tune.

steps: Play low E string fret 5 → should match open A string
Play A string fret 5 → should match open D string
Play D string fret 5 → should match open G string
Play G string fret 4 (not 5!) → should match open B string
Play B string fret 5 → should match open high E string

description: Chord quality quiz — identify chord types by their sound.
action: Play each chord and identify its quality (major, minor, or 7th) by ear before checking the name.

items.prompt: Play x02210 — this chord sounds: ___
items.hint: It has a sad, dark quality (Am)

items.prompt: Play 320003 — this chord sounds: ___
items.hint: It has a bright, happy quality (G)

items.prompt: Play x21200 — this chord sounds: ___
items.hint: It sounds bluesy and unresolved (B7)

items.prompt: Play xx0232 — this chord sounds: ___
items.hint: Bright and resolved (D)

items.prompt: Play 022000 — this chord sounds: ___
items.hint: Dark and melancholic (Em)

items.prompt: Play x32010 — this chord sounds: ___
items.hint: Full and bright (C)

description: Practice singing what you play — connect your voice to the fretboard.
action: Play a note on the guitar, then sing (or hum) that same pitch. Start with open strings. Then try fret 5 on each string. Then try random frets. The goal is to match the pitch with your voice.
context: Singing what you play is the fastest way to internalize pitch. It connects your inner ear to the fretboard. You don't need to be a good singer — just match the pitch.
constraint_note: Don't worry about singing quality. Just match the pitch. If you can't tell if you're matching, try humming instead — it's easier to control. Start with low notes (easier to match).

description: Learn the sound of common chord progressions.
action: Play each progression 3 times and memorize its emotional character: (1) I-IV-V (G-C-D): classic rock/folk. (2) I-V-vi-IV (G-D-Em-C): modern pop. (3) i-iv-v (Am-Dm-Em): minor/dark. (4) I-vi-IV-V (G-Em-C-D): 50s doo-wop.
context: Recognizing common progressions by ear lets you figure out songs quickly. Most popular music uses just a handful of progressions — learn to hear them and you can play along with almost anything.
constraint_note: After playing each progression, close your eyes and replay it in your head. Can you hear it without the guitar? That's the goal — internal hearing.

description: Music theory quiz — test your knowledge of notes, intervals, and chord construction.
action: Answer these music theory questions from memory.

items.prompt: How many half steps in an octave? ___
items.hint: Count every fret from open to fret 12

items.prompt: The notes with no sharp between them: E-F and ___
items.hint: The two pairs that are only 1 fret apart

items.prompt: A major chord is built from: root, major 3rd, and ___
items.hint: The 3 notes of a major triad

items.prompt: The relative minor of C major is: ___
items.hint: Same notes, different starting point

items.prompt: How many frets = a whole step? ___
items.hint: A half step is 1 fret

description: Interval identification on the guitar — play and name intervals.
action: On the low E string, play fret 5 (A), then play fret 7 (B). The interval is a major 2nd (2 frets). Now play fret 5 then fret 8 — minor 3rd (3 frets). Continue: identify the interval for each fret distance from 1 to 7 frets.
context: Hearing intervals on the guitar — not just knowing them theoretically — is what lets you transcribe music, improvise melodies, and communicate with other musicians.
constraint_note: Play both notes, then try to sing the interval. Can you hear the 'character' of each interval? Minor 2nd = tense. Perfect 5th = powerful. Each has a personality.

items.body: A minor second — the smallest interval. This is the 'Jaws' theme: that creeping half-step. Play fret 5 (A) then fret 6 (Bb). The tension is palpable. Minor seconds create dissonance and unease, which is why they're used in horror scores. On guitar, any two adjacent frets produce this interval.

A major second — a whole step. Play fret 5 (A) then fret 7 (B). This is the interval between most adjacent notes in a major scale (do-re). It sounds neutral and stepwise. The first two notes of 'Happy Birthday' form a major second. This is the most common distance you'll move when playing scales.

A minor third — the sad interval. Play fret 5 (A) then fret 8 (C). This melancholy sound is what makes A minor sad. The opening of 'Greensleeves' uses a minor third. Minor thirds are the building blocks of minor chords. Compare with 4 frets (major third) to hear the happy/sad difference.

A major third — the happy interval. Play fret 5 (A) then fret 9 (C#). Bright and cheerful. The first two notes of 'When the Saints Go Marching In.' The difference between major and minor — between happy and sad — is literally this one fret: 3 frets (minor) vs 4 frets (major). Train your ear to hear this.

A perfect fourth. Play fret 5 (A) then fret 10 (D). This is the 'Here Comes the Bride' interval. It's also the tuning interval between most adjacent strings. The fourth has a strong, open quality — not tense, not resolved, just... suspended. It's one of the easiest intervals to recognize by ear.

A tritone — the 'devil's interval.' Play fret 5 (A) then fret 11 (Eb). Medieval musicians called it 'diabolus in musica' because of its unsettling sound. The tritone divides the octave exactly in half. It's the opening of 'The Simpsons' theme and the tension note in dominant 7th chords. It wants desperately to resolve.

A perfect fifth. Play fret 5 (A) then fret 12 (E). The 'Star Wars' opening. The most consonant interval after the octave — stable, powerful, complete. This is the basis of power chords. The fifth is so fundamental it appears in virtually every musical tradition worldwide.

description: Practice finding a melody by ear — Happy Birthday.
action: Without looking up the notes, try to figure out 'Happy Birthday' on the B string by ear. Start on fret 0 (open B). Hum the melody, then find each note by trial and error. Write down the fret numbers as you find them.
context: Playing by ear is the ultimate musical skill. Starting with a melody you know well removes the 'what does it sound like?' question and lets you focus on 'where is that note?'
constraint_note: Don't look up the answer. The struggle IS the exercise. If you get stuck on a note, sing it, then slide your finger up the string until you find the matching fret.

description: Identify chord changes in a song by ear.
action: Think of a simple song you know (e.g., 'Let It Be', 'Knockin on Heaven's Door'). Without looking up chords, try to identify when the chords change and whether each chord sounds major or minor. Write down your guesses.
context: Identifying chord changes by ear is how musicians learn songs without sheet music. Start by just hearing WHEN the chord changes, then work on identifying WHAT it changes to.
constraint_note: Don't try to name the exact chord yet. Just identify: (1) when does the chord change? (2) does the new chord sound major or minor? (3) does it go up or down from the previous chord?

description: Learn the Nashville Number System basics.
action: Learn that chords in a key are numbered I through VII. In the key of G: I=G, ii=Am, iii=Bm, IV=C, V=D, vi=Em, vii°=F#dim. Uppercase = major, lowercase = minor. Play each chord and say its number.
context: The Nashville Number System lets you describe chord progressions in any key. 'I-V-vi-IV' means the same thing whether you're in G, C, or E. It's the universal language of chord progressions.
constraint_note: The pattern is always: I(major), ii(minor), iii(minor), IV(major), V(major), vi(minor), vii°(diminished). This pattern is the same in EVERY major key.

items.body: The I chord (G major) is 'home' — the tonic. Songs begin and end here. It feels resolved and stable. When you hear a song end on the I chord, your ear relaxes. The I chord defines the key: if a song keeps returning to G major, you're in the key of G. About 90% of pop songs end on the I chord.

The ii chord (A minor) adds gentle tension. It has a softer, contemplative quality. The ii-V-I progression is one of the most common in jazz and pop — 'Autumn Leaves' and countless standards use it. On guitar, Am is one of the first chords you learn, making ii-V-I accessible early.

The iii chord (B minor) is the most mysterious chord in a major key. It's used less frequently than I, IV, V, or vi, which makes it stand out. Radiohead and art-rock bands love the iii chord for its unexpected emotional color. It shares two notes with the I chord, so it can sometimes substitute for it.

The IV chord (C major) creates gentle forward motion away from home. The I-IV movement is the most common two-chord change in popular music. 'Let It Be,' 'No Woman No Cry,' and thousands of folk songs use I-IV as their foundation. It feels like taking a step forward — not tense, but not home either.

The V chord (D major) creates the strongest pull back to home. The V-I movement is the most powerful resolution in Western music — the 'authentic cadence.' The V chord contains F# which desperately wants to resolve up to G. This tension-and-release is what makes music feel like it's going somewhere.

The vi chord (E minor) is the 'sad relative' of I. It shares two notes with G major, which is why they sound related but emotionally different. The I-V-vi-IV progression (G-D-Em-C) is the most common in pop: 'Let It Be,' 'With or Without You,' 'Someone Like You' all use it.

The vii° chord (F# diminished) is the rarest and most unstable chord in a major key. It sounds tense and desperately wants to resolve to I. Most pop and rock songs avoid it, substituting V instead. But in jazz and classical, it appears as a passing chord. Think of it as a musical cliffhanger.

description: Key identification exercise — determine what key a progression is in.
action: For each progression, identify the key by finding which major scale contains all the chords.

items.prompt: G - C - D - Em is in the key of: ___
items.hint: G is the I chord, C is IV, D is V, Em is vi

items.prompt: C - Am - F - G is in the key of: ___
items.hint: C is I, Am is vi, F is IV, G is V

items.prompt: D - A - Bm - G is in the key of: ___
items.hint: D is I, A is V, Bm is vi, G is IV

items.prompt: A - E - F#m - D is in the key of: ___
items.hint: A is I, E is V, F#m is vi, D is IV

description: Advanced interval ear training — identify intervals without the guitar.
action: Hum or sing these intervals from any starting note: major 2nd (step up), major 3rd (bright skip), perfect 4th (Here Comes the Bride), perfect 5th (Star Wars), octave (Somewhere Over the Rainbow). Then try to identify intervals in songs you hear.
context: The ultimate ear training goal is hearing intervals without an instrument. When you can identify a perfect 5th just by hearing two notes, you can transcribe any melody.
constraint_note: Start by singing the song reference, then isolate just the first two notes. That's the interval. Practice until you can hear the interval without needing the song reference.

description: Transcription exercise — figure out a simple riff by ear.
action: Pick a simple, iconic guitar riff you know (Smoke on the Water, Seven Nation Army, Come As You Are). Without looking up tabs, figure it out by ear on your guitar. Hum it, then find each note.
context: Transcription — figuring out music by ear — is the most valuable musical skill. It combines interval recognition, fretboard knowledge, and musical memory into one exercise.
constraint_note: Start with the rhythm — tap it out first. Then find the first note. Then find the interval to the second note. Work note by note. It's slow at first but gets faster with practice.

description: Chord construction quiz — build chords from intervals.
action: Given a root note and chord type, name the notes in the chord.

items.prompt: C major chord (root, major 3rd, perfect 5th): C, ___, ___
items.hint: Major 3rd above C = E, Perfect 5th above C = G

items.prompt: A minor chord (root, minor 3rd, perfect 5th): A, ___, ___
items.hint: Minor 3rd above A = C, Perfect 5th above A = E

items.prompt: G major chord: G, ___, ___
items.hint: Major 3rd above G = B, Perfect 5th above G = D

items.prompt: D minor chord: D, ___, ___
items.hint: Minor 3rd above D = F, Perfect 5th above D = A

description: Practice the p-a-m-i reverse fingerpicking pattern.
action: Hold a Dm chord. Play: p (D string) → a (high E) → m (B) → i (G). This reverse pattern creates a descending cascade. Practice on Dm, Am, Em.
context: Reversing the finger order creates a completely different musical texture. Combining forward and reverse patterns gives you a full palette of fingerpicking sounds.
constraint_note: The ring finger (a) plucking right after the thumb feels unusual. Practice the p→a transition separately until it's smooth.

description: Practice fingerpicking with a capo — explore different keys and voicings.
action: Place a capo on fret 2. Play the same Am-C-G-Em fingerpicking pattern. Notice how the key shifts up but the finger shapes stay the same. Try capo on fret 4 and fret 7 too.
context: A capo lets you play in any key using open chord shapes. For fingerpicking, higher capo positions create a brighter, more delicate sound — great for intros and ballads.
constraint_note: The fingerpicking pattern is identical — only the pitch changes. Listen to how the same pattern sounds completely different at each capo position.

description: Practice harmonics — natural harmonics at frets 12, 7, and 5.
action: Lightly touch (don't press) the string directly above fret 12 and pluck. You should hear a bell-like harmonic. Try on all 6 strings. Then try fret 7 and fret 5 harmonics.
context: Harmonics are the bell-like tones produced by lightly touching the string at specific points. They're used for tuning, creating atmosphere, and adding sparkle to fingerstyle arrangements.
constraint_note: The key is LIGHT touch — barely graze the string directly above the fret wire, then lift your finger immediately after plucking. If you hear a thud, you're pressing too hard.

description: Learn the blues scale — the pentatonic with an added 'blue note'.
action: Play the Am blues scale: A-C-D-Eb-E-G-A. It's the Am pentatonic with one extra note (Eb, the 'blue note' at fret 8 on the G string). Play ascending and descending.
context: The blue note (flat 5th) is what gives blues its characteristic sound. Adding just this one note to the pentatonic transforms your solos from 'rock' to 'blues'.
constraint_note: The blue note creates tension — it wants to resolve to either the 4th (D) or the 5th (E). Don't linger on it; use it as a passing tone between D and E.

description: Practice scale patterns in thirds — a musical way to practice scales.
action: Play the C major scale in thirds: C-E, D-F, E-G, F-A, G-B, A-C, B-D. Play each pair as two separate notes ascending. This creates a musical, melodic sound.
context: Scales in thirds sound like actual music, not exercises. Many melodies are built on thirds. Practicing this way trains your ear and fingers simultaneously.
constraint_note: Each pair should be played smoothly — the two notes connected, not choppy. Use alternate picking. This is harder than straight scale runs.

description: Learn the CAGED system — 5 chord shapes that map the entire fretboard.
action: The 5 open chord shapes (C, A, G, E, D) can be moved up the neck as barre chords to play any chord. Play C major using: open C shape, A shape (barre at fret 3), G shape (barre at fret 5), E shape (barre at fret 8), D shape (barre at fret 10).
context: CAGED is the master system for understanding the guitar fretboard. It shows that the same chord exists in 5 different positions, connected across the entire neck.
constraint_note: You already know the E-shape and A-shape barres. The G, C, and D shapes as barres are harder and less common, but understanding the system is what matters.

description: Practice the major scale in two octaves — position shifting.
action: Play the G major scale from low E fret 3 (G) all the way up to high E fret 3 (G, two octaves higher). This requires shifting hand position mid-scale. Practice the shift point until it's seamless.
context: Playing scales across multiple octaves requires position shifts — moving your hand up or down the neck mid-scale. Smooth shifts are essential for fluid lead playing.
constraint_note: The shift should be invisible to the listener. No gap, no extra noise. Slide into the new position on a note that's common to both positions.

description: Fretboard mapping exercise — find every occurrence of one note.
action: Pick the note A. Find every A on the fretboard (there are at least 12 within the first 12 frets). Play each one. Then pick another note (C, G, E) and repeat.
context: Knowing where every instance of a note lives across all 6 strings is what lets you navigate the fretboard freely instead of being stuck in one position.
constraint_note: Start with the E and A strings (you know these). Then use octave shapes to find the note on D, G, B, and high E strings. There's a pattern.

description: Practice strumming with chord embellishments — hammer-ons and pull-offs within chords.
action: Play a G chord with steady strumming. While strumming, hammer-on your pinky to the A string fret 3, then pull off. This adds a melodic movement within the chord. Try on C (hammer B fret 3) and D (hammer high E fret 3).
context: Chord embellishments — adding and removing fingers while strumming — is what makes acoustic guitar sound alive. It's the secret sauce of singer-songwriters.
constraint_note: The strumming pattern doesn't change. The hammer-on/pull-off happens between strums. It should sound like a subtle melodic movement within the chord, not a separate event.

description: Practice the 'island strum' — the classic reggae/pop strumming pattern.
action: Play this pattern: D - U - U D U (down, miss, up, miss, up, down, up). It has a bouncy, island feel. Practice on C-G-Am-F progression.
context: This pattern is used in countless pop songs (Jason Mraz, Jack Johnson, Bob Marley). Its bouncy feel comes from the two consecutive upstrokes.

reference.body:
Beat:  1   &   2   &   3   &   4   &
Strum: D   -   U   -   U   D   U   -
       ↓       ↑       ↑   ↓   ↑

description: Practice percussive slap strumming — add a snare-like hit to your strumming.
action: While strumming, on beats 2 and 4, slap the strings with the side of your thumb immediately after the strum. This creates a percussive 'crack' that mimics a snare drum.
context: Percussive strumming turns your guitar into a rhythm section — guitar + drums in one. It's a signature technique of acoustic performers who play solo.
constraint_note: The slap should be sharp and quick — hit the strings against the fretboard with the fleshy part of your thumb, then immediately release. It takes practice to get the timing right.

description: Practice strumming with a swing feel — triplet-based rhythm.
action: Instead of straight eighth notes (even spacing), play with a swing: the downbeat is long (~2/3) and the upbeat is short (~1/3). Strum D-U with this uneven feel on a 12-bar blues in A.
context: Swing feel is the rhythmic foundation of blues, jazz, and early rock and roll. It's what makes music 'groove' instead of march.
constraint_note: Think 'daa-da, daa-da' instead of 'da-da, da-da'. The first note of each pair is longer. It should feel lazy and relaxed, not rushed.

description: Practice counting and playing in 6/8 time.
action: Count: 1-2-3-4-5-6, 1-2-3-4-5-6. Accent beats 1 and 4. Strum: D on 1, U on 2-3, D on 4, U on 5-6. Play on Am-G-F-E progression. This is the feel of 'Nothing Else Matters' and many ballads.
context: 6/8 time has a flowing, circular feel — like a slow waltz with subdivisions. It's common in ballads, Irish music, and emotional rock songs.

reference.body:
Beat:  1   2   3   4   5   6
Strum: D   U   U   D   U   U
       ↓   ↑   ↑   ↓   ↑   ↑

description: Rhythm dictation — write down the rhythm you hear.
action: Tap a random rhythm on a table (8 beats). Then try to write it down using D (tap) and - (rest). Example: D-D--D-D = tap, tap, rest, rest, tap, rest, tap. Do 5 different rhythms.
context: Rhythm dictation trains you to hear and notate rhythms — a skill that helps with learning songs by ear and communicating rhythms to other musicians.
constraint_note: Start simple: 4-beat patterns with only quarter notes and rests. Then add eighth notes. The goal is to accurately capture what you hear.

description: Practice identifying the root note of a chord by ear.
action: Play a chord (any chord you know). Then try to sing or hum the lowest note (the root). Check by playing just the bass string. Can you match the root? Try with 10 different chords.
context: Identifying the root note is the first step to identifying chords by ear. The root is the 'home base' of the chord — the note everything else is built on.
constraint_note: The root is usually the lowest note in the chord. Strum the chord, then hum what you think is the lowest pitch. Check by playing just the bass note alone.

description: Practice call and response — play a phrase, then answer it.
action: Play a short phrase (3-5 notes) from the Am pentatonic. Then play an 'answer' phrase that responds to it musically. The answer should feel like a conversation — related but different. Do 10 call-and-response pairs.
context: Call and response is the foundation of blues soloing and musical conversation. It trains you to think in phrases rather than random notes, which is what makes solos musical.
constraint_note: The 'answer' should relate to the 'call' — similar rhythm but different notes, or similar notes but different rhythm. Think of it as a musical conversation.

description: Learn to hear the difference between major 7th and dominant 7th chords.
action: Play Cmaj7 (x32000) then C7 (x32310). Listen to the difference. Cmaj7 sounds dreamy and smooth. C7 sounds bluesy and wants to resolve. Play each pair 5 times: Cmaj7/C7, Amaj7/A7, Dmaj7/D7.
context: Major 7th and dominant 7th chords sound very different despite both being '7th chords'. Hearing this distinction is essential for jazz, blues, and sophisticated pop.
constraint_note: Cmaj7 sounds 'pretty' and stable. C7 sounds 'tense' and wants to move to F. This tension-resolution is the engine of blues and jazz harmony.

description: Relative pitch exercise — sing intervals before playing them.
action: Play any note on the guitar. Before playing the next note, SING the interval you want (e.g., sing a perfect 5th up). Then play it and check. Were you right? Do 20 intervals: mix of 2nds, 3rds, 4ths, 5ths.
context: Singing before playing is the ultimate ear training exercise. It proves you can hear the interval internally, not just recognize it after the fact.
constraint_note: It's okay to be wrong — that's how you learn. The goal is to close the gap between what you sing and what the guitar plays. Over time, your accuracy improves dramatically.

description: Practice identifying chord progressions in songs you listen to.
action: Pick any song you're listening to. Try to identify: (1) Is it major or minor key? (2) How many different chords are there? (3) Can you hear the I chord (home base)? (4) Can you hear the V chord (tension)? Write down your observations.
context: Analyzing music you already enjoy is the most natural form of ear training. You're not studying — you're just listening more carefully to music you love.
constraint_note: Don't try to name exact chords yet. Just notice: when does the chord change? Does it go up or down? Does it feel like tension or resolution? These observations build your ear.

description: Music theory — understand the circle of fifths.
action: The circle of fifths: C→G→D→A→E→B→F#→Db→Ab→Eb→Bb→F→C. Each step adds one sharp (going clockwise) or one flat (counterclockwise). Play each note on the E string and hear the 5th relationship.
context: The circle of fifths is the master map of music theory. It shows how all 12 keys relate to each other and predicts which chords naturally go together in any key.
constraint_note: You don't need to memorize the whole circle. Just understand: keys next to each other on the circle share the most notes. G and D are close (1 note different). G and F# are far (6 notes different).

items.reveal: C D E F G A B
items.body: C major is the 'white keys' key — no sharps, no flats. It's the starting point of the circle of fifths and the default key for learning theory. On guitar, C major uses one of the first chord shapes you learn. Every other key is defined by how many sharps or flats it adds relative to C.

Moving clockwise, G major adds one sharp: F#. This is the most popular key for acoustic guitar because open chord shapes (G, C, D, Em, Am) fall naturally under the fingers. Knowing G has F# helps when figuring out melodies by ear — avoid natural F, use F# instead.

D major adds C# to G's F#. It's a bright, ringing key — the open D and A strings resonate naturally. Many country and folk songs use D major. When soloing, avoid natural F and C — they'll clash with D major chords. The D major scale has a comfortable box shape starting at fret 2.

A major has three sharps: F#, C#, G#. Popular for rock and blues — AC/DC's 'Back in Black' is in A. The open A string provides a powerful bass foundation. The A major pentatonic scale (A-B-C#-E-F#) is one of the most useful for soloing over A major progressions.

F major has one flat: Bb. Moving counter-clockwise on the circle adds flats instead of sharps. F major is common in jazz and R&B. On guitar, F major requires a barre chord at fret 1 — one of the first barre chords beginners tackle. The Bb means every B in the key is lowered by one fret.

Bb major has two flats: Bb and Eb. It's a common key for brass instruments (trumpet, trombone) and appears frequently in jazz and New Orleans music. On guitar, Bb requires a barre chord. Many guitarists use a capo on fret 1 and play A-shaped chords to avoid the barre. Two flats means B and E are both lowered.

description: Practice playing by ear — figure out a simple song's chords.
action: Pick a simple song (3-4 chords). Hum the melody. Find the key by identifying the 'home' chord. Then try common progressions in that key until one matches. Write down the chords you find.
context: This is the real-world application of all ear training skills combined: key identification, chord quality recognition, and progression pattern matching.
constraint_note: Start with songs you KNOW use simple chords (folk, early rock, pop). Try the I-V-vi-IV pattern first — it fits a huge number of songs. If it doesn't fit, try I-IV-V or i-iv-v.

description: Speed and accuracy test — play the chromatic exercise as fast as possible cleanly.
action: Play the 1-2-3-4 chromatic exercise across all 6 strings. Start at your comfortable tempo. Increase by 5 BPM every 30 seconds. Record your maximum clean tempo. This is your benchmark — try to beat it next session.
context: Tracking your maximum clean tempo over time is the most objective measure of technical progress. It's motivating to see the number go up week by week.
constraint_note: 'Clean' means every note rings clearly, timing is even, and no notes are missed. The moment any note buzzes or timing slips, that's your max. Record it.

description: Full practice routine — combine technique, chords, scales, and rhythm in one session.
action: Complete this mini practice routine: (1) Hand stretches — 1 min. (2) Chromatic 1-2-3-4 warm-up — 2 min. (3) Chord transitions (pick any 4 chords) — 3 min. (4) Scale run (Am pentatonic) — 3 min. (5) Strumming pattern on a progression — 3 min. (6) Free play — 3 min.
context: A structured practice routine ensures balanced development across all skill areas. This mini routine covers the essentials in 15 minutes — perfect for a Forge session.
constraint_note: Use a timer for each section. Don't let any section run over — the discipline of moving on is part of the practice. The 'free play' at the end is your reward.

steps: Hand stretches and finger warm-up (1 min)
Chromatic 1-2-3-4 exercise with metronome (2 min)
Chord transition drill — 4 chords, 1-minute changes (3 min)
Am pentatonic scale — ascending, descending, sequences (3 min)
Strumming pattern practice on G-C-D-Em (3 min)
Free play — play anything you want (3 min)

description: Practice the classic blues lick in Am pentatonic — the most used lick in rock guitar.
action: Learn this essential blues lick. Play it slowly, then gradually speed up. This lick uses a bend on the G string and resolves to the root note A.

description: Practice the Smoke on the Water riff — the most famous guitar riff ever.
action: Learn this iconic riff. It uses power chord shapes (two notes at a time). Play it slowly until the rhythm is locked in, then speed up.

description: Practice the Seven Nation Army riff — a simple but powerful single-note riff.
action: Learn this riff. It's all on the A string — perfect for beginners. Focus on the rhythm: the spacing between notes is what makes it recognizable.

description: Practice the Come As You Are riff — a clean, melodic single-string pattern.
action: Learn this Nirvana riff. It's played on the low E string with a clean tone. The pattern mirrors itself — ascending then descending.

description: Practice the 12-bar blues shuffle riff in E — the backbone of blues guitar.
action: Learn the classic blues shuffle pattern. Your index finger holds the power chord while your pinky adds the shuffle note 2 frets up. Play on E, A, and B strings following the 12-bar form.

description: Practice the pentatonic run — ascending and descending with alternate picking.
action: Play this Am pentatonic run across all 6 strings. Use strict alternate picking. Start slow and build speed gradually.

description: Practice the classic rock ending lick — a must-know phrase for jamming.
action: Learn this classic turnaround lick in A. It descends chromatically on the B and high E strings while the A bass note drones. Used to end blues and rock songs.

description: Practice the basic fingerpicking pattern for House of the Rising Sun.
action: Learn this arpeggiated pattern in Am. The thumb alternates bass notes while fingers pick the treble strings in sequence. This is the pattern behind 'House of the Rising Sun'.

description: Practice the Travis picking pattern in C — alternating bass with melody.
action: Learn this Travis picking pattern on a C chord. The thumb alternates between the A and D strings while the index and middle fingers pick melody notes on the treble strings.

description: Practice the Blackbird fingerpicking intro pattern.
action: Learn this Beatles-inspired fingerpicking pattern. It uses a moving bass line with open strings ringing on top. The key is letting notes sustain into each other.

description: Practice the Stairway to Heaven intro arpeggio pattern.
action: Learn this iconic descending arpeggio pattern. The bass line descends chromatically (A → G# → G → F#) while the treble strings create a flowing melody above.

description: Practice the spider exercise with string skipping — advanced coordination drill.
action: Play this string-skipping spider pattern. Instead of moving to adjacent strings, you skip one string each time. This dramatically improves right-hand accuracy.

description: Practice the minor pentatonic lick with hammer-ons and pull-offs.
action: Learn this legato lick in Am pentatonic. Use hammer-ons (h) and pull-offs (p) where marked — only pick the first note on each string.

description: Practice the basic country chicken-picking pattern.
action: Learn this hybrid picking pattern (pick + fingers). The pick plays the bass note, then your middle finger snaps a treble string. The 'snap' gives it the characteristic country twang.

description: Practice the D-U-D-U picking exercise across two strings — build string crossing accuracy.
action: Alternate pick between two adjacent strings. Start with the B and high E strings at fret 5. Down on B, up on E, down on B, up on E. Keep it perfectly even.

description: Practice the classic rock power chord riff pattern.
action: Learn this driving power chord riff. Palm mute the chugging parts (PM) and let the accented chords ring. This is the foundation of rock rhythm guitar.

description: Practice the natural harmonics chime pattern — a beautiful ambient technique.
action: Play natural harmonics at fret 12 across all strings, then at fret 7, then at fret 5. Let each harmonic ring into the next. This creates a bell-like, ethereal sound.

description: Practice the Am-G-F-E descending bass line with arpeggios.
action: Play this descending chord progression with fingerpicked arpeggios. The bass note descends step by step (A→G→F→E) while the treble pattern stays consistent.

description: Practice the pentatonic sequence in groups of 4 — a classic lead guitar pattern.
action: Play the Am pentatonic in groups of 4 notes: 1-2-3-4, 2-3-4-5, 3-4-5-6, etc. This creates a flowing, musical run that sounds much better than straight scale playing.

description: Practice the Dust in the Wind fingerpicking pattern — the classic Travis pick exercise.
action: Learn this iconic fingerpicking pattern from Kansas. The thumb alternates bass notes while fingers create a flowing melody. It's one of the best Travis picking exercises ever written.

description: Learn Am pentatonic box 3 — the C-shape position (frets 9-13).
action: Learn pentatonic box 3. This box sits at frets 9-13 and connects directly above box 2. The root notes (A) are on the A string fret 12 and B string fret 10. Play ascending and descending 5 times.
context: Box 3 is the 'C-shape' pentatonic — it lives around where a C-shape barre chord would be. With boxes 1, 2, and 3 you now cover frets 5-13, more than half the neck.
constraint_note: The G string has a wide stretch (fret 9 to fret 12 — 3 frets). Use your index finger on fret 9 and pinky on fret 12. This stretch gets easier with practice.
example: Root notes to emphasize: A string fret 12 (A) and B string fret 10 (A). Start and end your runs on these notes to keep the scale grounded in the key.

description: Learn Am pentatonic box 4 — the A-shape position (frets 12-15).
action: Learn pentatonic box 4. This box sits at frets 12-15 and connects above box 3. The root notes (A) are on the A string fret 12 and G string fret 14. Play ascending and descending 5 times.
context: Box 4 is the 'A-shape' pentatonic — it wraps around where an A-shape barre chord sits. Notice that fret 12 is the octave — the same notes as the open strings. This box shares its bottom notes with box 3.
constraint_note: Frets 12-15 are close together, so the stretches are smaller than lower positions. This box should feel physically easier than boxes 1-3. Focus on clean tone.
example: Root notes: A string fret 12 (A) and G string fret 14 (A). The A string fret 12 is shared with box 3 — this is your pivot point between the two boxes.

description: Learn Am pentatonic box 5 — the G-shape position (frets 2-5). This box connects back to box 1.
action: Learn pentatonic box 5. This box sits at frets 2-5 and connects BELOW box 1 — completing the circle. The root notes (A) are on the G string fret 2 and high E string fret 5. Play ascending and descending 5 times.
context: Box 5 is the 'G-shape' pentatonic. It's special because it connects the bottom of the neck back to box 1 at fret 5. With all 5 boxes, the pentatonic pattern tiles the ENTIRE fretboard with no gaps.
constraint_note: The top notes of box 5 (fret 5) are the same as the bottom notes of box 1 (fret 5). Play box 5 ascending, then continue directly into box 1 without stopping — feel the seamless connection.
example: Root notes: G string fret 2 (A) and high E string fret 5 (A). After learning this box, try: box 5 (frets 2-5) → box 1 (frets 5-8) → box 2 (frets 7-10). Three boxes connected!

description: Discover the major pentatonic — the Am pentatonic shape IS the C major pentatonic.
action: Play the Am pentatonic box 1 (frets 5-8). Now play it again, but this time start and end on C (A string fret 3, or high E string fret 8) instead of A. Listen to how the same notes sound happy/bright instead of sad/dark. You just played C major pentatonic.
context: Am pentatonic and C major pentatonic contain the exact same 5 notes: A, C, D, E, G. The only difference is which note you treat as 'home.' Start on A = minor sound. Start on C = major sound. This relative major/minor relationship is one of the most powerful concepts in guitar.
constraint_note: Play the scale twice back to back: first starting/ending on A (fret 5 on low E), then starting/ending on C (fret 8 on low E). Same fingers, same shape, completely different mood. Let your ear hear the difference.
example: Minor feel: start on A (E string fret 5), play up the box, end on A. Major feel: start on C (A string fret 3), play up the box, end on C (high E fret 8). Same 5 notes, different home base.

description: Play G major pentatonic using the Em pentatonic shape — same relationship, different key.
action: Play the Em pentatonic box 1 (open position). Now play it starting and ending on G (E string fret 3) instead of E. This is G major pentatonic. Strum a G chord, then play the scale over it — hear how bright and happy it sounds.
context: Em pentatonic = G major pentatonic. Same pattern as Am/C major — the minor shape is always the major pentatonic of the note 3 frets lower. Em (fret 0) → G major (fret 3). This works for every key.
constraint_note: Strum a G chord 4 times, then play the G major pentatonic scale, then strum G again. The scale should sound like it 'belongs' over the chord. If it sounds wrong, you're probably starting on E instead of G.
example: G major pentatonic notes: G, A, B, D, E. Play: G(E string fret 3) → A(fret 5) → B(fret 7) → continue up through the Em box shape.

description: Play D major pentatonic using the Bm pentatonic shape — reinforce the pattern.
action: Play the Bm pentatonic box 1 at fret 7. Now start and end on D (A string fret 5, or E string fret 10) instead of B. This is D major pentatonic. Strum a D chord, then play the scale. The universal rule: any minor pentatonic shape IS the major pentatonic 3 frets lower.
context: Bm pentatonic (fret 7) = D major pentatonic. The pattern is now proven in 3 keys: Am=C major, Em=G major, Bm=D major. You'll never need to learn separate major pentatonic shapes — just use the minor shape you already know and shift your 'home' note.
constraint_note: The rule to memorize: to play [X] major pentatonic, play [X]'s relative minor pentatonic shape. The relative minor is always 3 frets HIGHER. D major → Bm (3 frets up from D). G major → Em. C major → Am.
example: D major pentatonic notes: D, E, F#, A, B. These are the same notes as Bm pentatonic. Play the Bm box 1 shape at fret 7, but resolve to D instead of B.

description: CAGED scale system: E-shape chord + pentatonic box 1 — see how the scale wraps around the chord.
action: Play the Am barre chord (E-shape at fret 5). Now play pentatonic box 1 around it. Notice: the chord tones (A, C, E) are INSIDE the scale pattern. The chord is the skeleton, the scale is the full picture. Exercise: strum Am chord → play box 1 up → play box 1 down → strum Am chord.
context: This is the first CAGED-scale connection. The E-shape barre chord and pentatonic box 1 occupy the same fret territory. When you solo in box 1, you're playing notes that orbit around the chord shape you already know.
constraint_note: After playing the scale, land on a chord tone (A on fret 5, C on G string fret 5, E on B string fret 5). These notes sound 'resolved' because they're part of the chord. The other scale notes (D, G) create movement and tension.

description: CAGED scale system: D-shape chord + pentatonic box 2.
action: Play the Am chord using the D-shape at fret 7: index barres D-G-B at fret 7, pinky on B string fret 10. Now play pentatonic box 2 around it. Exercise: play chord → play box 2 up → play box 2 down → play chord.
context: The D-shape is less common as a barre chord, but understanding it unlocks box 2. When you're soloing in box 2 (frets 7-10), the D-shape chord is hiding inside those same frets.
constraint_note: The D-shape barre is awkward — that's okay. The point isn't to use it as a chord in songs, but to SEE the chord shape within the scale pattern. This visual connection is what makes the fretboard logical.

description: CAGED scale system: C-shape chord + pentatonic box 3.
action: Play the Am chord using the C-shape: think of an open C chord shape, but shifted so the root lands on A string fret 12. Now play pentatonic box 3 around it. Exercise: play chord → play box 3 up → play box 3 down → play chord.
context: The C-shape barre is the hardest CAGED shape to play as a chord. But you don't need to play it as a chord — you need to SEE it within box 3. When soloing in box 3, the C-shape chord tones are your anchor points.
constraint_note: If the C-shape barre is too hard, just fret the root (A string fret 12) and the notes you can reach. The goal is recognizing the shape within the scale, not perfecting the barre.

description: CAGED scale system: A-shape chord + pentatonic box 4.
action: Play the Am chord using the A-shape barre at fret 12: index barres at fret 12, other fingers form the Am open shape above it. Now play pentatonic box 4 around it. Exercise: play chord → play box 4 up → play box 4 down → play chord.
context: The A-shape barre at fret 12 is the same Am chord one octave higher than the open Am. Box 4 wraps around this familiar shape. This is often the easiest CAGED connection to see because the open Am shape is so well-known.
constraint_note: At fret 12, the frets are close together — the A-shape barre is physically easier here than at lower frets. Focus on hearing how box 4 sounds different from box 1 even though it's the same key.

description: CAGED scale system: G-shape chord + pentatonic box 5 — completing the circle.
action: Play the Am chord using the G-shape: think of an open G chord shape shifted so the root lands on G string fret 2 and E string fret 5. Now play pentatonic box 5 around it. This box connects back to box 1 — the CAGED system is a complete loop.
context: The G-shape is rarely played as a barre chord, but it's the key to understanding box 5. With all 5 CAGED shapes mapped to all 5 pentatonic boxes, you now have a complete system: every fret on the neck belongs to a chord shape AND a scale pattern. No more guessing.
constraint_note: The big picture: E-shape→box1→D-shape→box2→C-shape→box3→A-shape→box4→G-shape→box5→back to E-shape. This cycle repeats every 12 frets. You now own the entire fretboard.

description: Move pentatonic box 1 to a new key — Am (fret 5) vs Dm (fret 10). Same shape, different position.
action: Play Am pentatonic box 1 at fret 5. Now play the EXACT same shape starting at fret 10. This is Dm pentatonic. The shape is identical — only the starting fret changed. The root note on the low E string tells you the key: fret 5 = A, fret 10 = D.
context: This is the most powerful concept in guitar: shapes are moveable. Every pentatonic box, every chord shape, every scale pattern works at ANY fret. The fret position determines the key. Learn one shape, play in 12 keys.
constraint_note: Play both positions back to back. They should feel identical under your fingers. The only difference is pitch — Dm sounds higher than Am. Your muscle memory from box 1 at fret 5 transfers directly to fret 10.

description: Practice pentatonic box 1 in two more keys — Gm (fret 3) and Cm (fret 8).
action: Play box 1 at fret 3 (Gm pentatonic) — ascending and descending twice. Then move to fret 8 (Cm pentatonic) — ascending and descending twice. Same shape both times. Then play Am (fret 5) to confirm it's the same pattern everywhere.
context: By now you've played box 1 at frets 3, 5, 8, and 10 — four different keys with zero new shapes to learn. This is why guitar is a pattern-based instrument: learn the pattern once, apply it everywhere.
constraint_note: At fret 3 (Gm), the frets are wider apart — the stretch is harder. At fret 8 (Cm), the frets are closer — it's physically easier. Notice how the same shape feels different at different positions.

description: Key identification quiz — name the key from the pentatonic box 1 position.
action: For each fret position, name the minor pentatonic key. The root is on the low E string at the starting fret.
context: Knowing which key you're in based on fret position is essential for jamming with other musicians. Someone says 'solo in Em' — you need to instantly know where to put your hand.

description: Scale-chord connection: play Am chord, then find the chord tones inside the Am pentatonic.
action: Strum an Am chord. The chord tones are A, C, and E. Now play pentatonic box 1 slowly, and PAUSE on each chord tone: A (E string fret 5), C (G string fret 5), E (B string fret 5). These are the 'strong' notes — they sound resolved. The other notes (D, G) create movement.
context: This is why some notes in a solo sound 'right' and others sound 'passing.' The chord tones (root, 3rd, 5th) are the anchor points. The other pentatonic notes (2nd, 4th/7th) create tension that resolves when you land on a chord tone.
constraint_note: Play the scale and hold each chord tone for 2 beats, but play the non-chord tones as quick passing notes. Hear how the chord tones feel like 'home' and the others feel like 'traveling.'
example: In box 1: A is at E5, D7, e5. C is at A3 (just below the box), G5. E is at D2 (below box), B5, open high E. When soloing, aim to land on A, C, or E on strong beats.

description: Scale-chord connection: play G chord, then find chord tones inside G major pentatonic.
action: Strum a G chord. Chord tones: G, B, D. Now play the G major pentatonic (= Em pentatonic shape, open position) and pause on each chord tone: G (E string fret 3), B (A string fret 2), D (D string open). These are your anchor notes when soloing over G.
context: This exercise proves the pattern is universal: in ANY key, the chord tones are the strong notes within the scale. Learn to see chord tones inside scale patterns and your solos will always sound musical, not random.
constraint_note: Strum G → play scale emphasizing G, B, D → strum G. The scale should feel like an extension of the chord, not a separate thing. This connection is what makes great soloists sound like they're 'playing the changes.'

description: Box transition exercise: smoothly connect box 1 → box 2 through the overlap zone.
action: Ascend through Am pentatonic box 1 (frets 5-8). At the top of box 1, pivot into box 2 using the shared notes at frets 7-8. Continue ascending through box 2 (frets 7-10). Then descend back: box 2 down → pivot → box 1 down.
context: The overlap zone between boxes is where the magic happens. Boxes 1 and 2 share notes at frets 7-8 on multiple strings. These shared notes are your bridge — you can seamlessly shift position without the listener hearing a 'jump.'
constraint_note: The transition should be invisible. No pause, no position shift noise, no change in volume. Practice the pivot point (around frets 7-8) separately until it's smooth, then play the full run.

description: Full neck pentatonic run: ascend box 1→2→3, then descend 3→2→1.
action: Play the Am pentatonic ascending through 3 connected boxes: start at box 1 (fret 5), transition to box 2 (fret 7-8), transition to box 3 (fret 10). Then descend all the way back. This covers frets 5-13 — over half the neck in one continuous run.
context: This is the payoff of learning individual boxes and transitions. A 3-box run gives you enough range for expressive solos that move up and down the neck instead of being trapped in one position.
constraint_note: Go VERY slowly at first. The transitions are the hard part. Once you can play it cleanly at 40 BPM, increase by 5 BPM. A clean slow run sounds better than a sloppy fast one.

description: Reference: Capo transposition chart — find the right capo position for any key.
action: Use this chart to find where to place your capo. If you know a song in G shapes but need to play in A, put the capo on fret 2. The chart shows what key you get when playing common chord shapes with a capo at each fret.
context: This is the most googled guitar reference. Bookmark this task — you'll come back to it whenever you need to change the key of a song.

description: Reference: How to change guitar strings — step by step.
action: Follow these steps to change your guitar strings. Change strings one at a time to maintain neck tension. New strings need stretching — they'll go out of tune for a day or two.
context: Every guitarist needs to change strings regularly. Fresh strings sound brighter and stay in tune better. Change them every 1-3 months depending on how much you play.

steps: Loosen the old string by turning the tuning peg until it's slack
Unwind the string from the tuning peg and remove it from the bridge pin (acoustic) or through the back (electric)
Insert the new string through the bridge and pull it up to the tuning peg
Thread the string through the tuning peg hole, leaving about 3 inches of slack
Wind the string by turning the peg — wind DOWNWARD on the peg so the string wraps neatly
Tune to pitch using a tuner. The string will stretch and go flat — retune several times
Stretch the string: gently pull it away from the fretboard at the 12th fret, then retune. Repeat until it holds pitch
Trim the excess string at the tuning peg with wire cutters

description: Reference: How to use a capo — what it does and where to place it.
action: A capo clamps across all strings at a fret, raising the pitch. It lets you play open chord shapes in any key. Place it just behind the fret wire (toward the headstock), not on top of it. Always retune after placing the capo.
context: A capo is the most useful guitar accessory. It lets you play songs in any key using the chord shapes you already know, without learning barre chords.

steps: A capo raises all strings by the same amount — capo on fret 2 raises everything by 2 semitones
Place the capo just behind the fret wire, pressing firmly and evenly across all 6 strings
Check each string after placing — if any buzz, adjust the capo position
Always retune after placing the capo — it can slightly alter string tension
To find the right capo position: if you know the song in G shapes but need key of A, capo on fret 2 (G + 2 = A)
Remove the capo when not needed — leaving it on can wear the frets and affect intonation

description: Reference: Drop D tuning guide — tune your low E string down to D.
action: Drop D tuning: tune your low E string down one whole step to D. Everything else stays the same: D-A-D-G-B-E. This lets you play power chords with one finger and gives a heavier, deeper sound.
context: Drop D is the most common alternate tuning. Used in rock (Foo Fighters, Rage Against the Machine), metal, and folk. It makes power chords easier and adds bass depth.

steps: Start in standard tuning (E-A-D-G-B-E)
Tune the low E string DOWN until it matches the D string (4th string) but one octave lower
Check: play the low string at fret 7 — it should match the open A string
In Drop D, power chords are played with one finger barring the bottom 3 strings
To return to standard: tune the low string UP until fret 5 matches the open A string
Songs in Drop D: Everlong (Foo Fighters), Killing in the Name (RATM), Dear Prudence (Beatles)

description: Reference: Open G tuning guide — the blues and slide guitar tuning.
action: Open G tuning: D-G-D-G-B-D. Strum all open strings and you get a G major chord. This is the tuning for blues slide guitar, Rolling Stones, and many folk songs.
context: Open G is the second most common alternate tuning. Keith Richards (Rolling Stones) plays almost exclusively in Open G. It's also the standard tuning for slide guitar and banjo-style playing.

steps: Start in standard tuning (E-A-D-G-B-E)
Tune the low E string DOWN to D (same as Drop D)
Keep the A string as is — wait, actually tune A DOWN to G (match the 3rd string G)
Keep D string as is
Keep G string as is
Keep B string as is
Tune the high E string DOWN to D (match the 4th string D, but higher octave)
Final tuning: D-G-D-G-B-D — strum open strings to hear a G major chord
Songs in Open G: Start Me Up (Rolling Stones), Tumbling Dice, many Delta blues songs
